## Page 3

Hi.
PREFACE.
. This volume has been prepared in accordance with the provisions of
Section 6 (1) of the Census Act (Northern Ireland), 1925. The 1926 Census
statistics which it contains were compiled from the returns made as at midnight

    of the 18-19th April, 1926 : they supersede those in the Preliminary Report
published in August, 1926, and may be regarded as :final.
The Census publications will consist of:-
1.
SEVEN COUNTY VoLUMES, each similar in design and scope to the present
publication.
·
2.
A GENERAL REPORT relating to Northern Ireland as a whole, covering
in more detail the statistics shown in the County Volumes, and
containing in addition tables showing
(i.) the occupational distribution of persons engaged in each of
51 groups of industries ;
(ii.) the distribution of the foreign born population by nationality,
age, marital condition, and occupation;
(iii.) the distribution of families of dependent children under 16
years of age, by age, sex, marital condition, and occupation of
parent;
(iv.) the occupational distribution of persons suffering from infirmities.
3.
A TOPOGRAPHICAL INDEX, dealing in separate alphabetical sections with
each type of area for which statistics are published in the County
series. The particulars given for each territorial unit include references
to the 6" Ordnance Survey rna ps and to Tables 3, 4, or 5 of the
relative County Volume.
The net prices for the above publications will be 5s. Od. for each County
Volume and lOs. Od. each for the General Report and the Topographical Index.
A complete set of these volumes may be
obtained directly from the
Superintendent, H.M. Stationery Office, 15, Donegall Square West, Belfast, at the
special inclusive price of £2 2s. Od. strictly net.
The Registrar-General is empowered by Section 6 (2) of t)le Census Act
(Northern Ireland)_, 1925, to prepare and supply from the Census returns statistics
other than those published in the Census Reports, at the request and cost of any
local authority or person.
Any special demands for statistics more detailed than
those published (either as regards subject matter or area) can thus, as far as office
records permit, be satisfied.
Requests for special compilation of Census statistics
should be addressed to the Registrar-General, who will be pleased to ad vise and
furnish an estimate of cost.
APRIL,. 1928.'
GENERAL REGISTER OFFICE,
M'QRRAY: STREET,
BELFAST.

## Page 6

Vl.
PART I.
DEFINITIONS AND EXPLANATIONS OF
TERMS USED IN THIS VOLUME.
AREA.
The acreages given are as measured by the Ordnance Survey of 1899-1907.
They exclude :-
In Tables I, 3-5 (a) Foreshore and unnamed islands of less than one rood
in extent.
In Tables 3-5
(b) The Maiden's Islands in the North Channel.
(c) The larger rivers, lakes and tidewa ys.
N.B.-While (b) and (c) above are not enclosed within townland boundaries
they form part of administrative areas. The extent of these excluded portions is
shown for .the county, for county districts, and for poor law unions in foot-notes
to the appropriate tables.
·
POPULATION.
The population comprises all persons, including Forces of the Crown, enumer-

    ated as at midnight of Sunday the 18th-19th April, 1926, together with those who,
not having been enumerated elsewhere, arrived in port or were received into any
dwelling on the morning of the 19th April before the Census returns were collected.
The population assigned to each area is that actually present (as defined
above) within it, whether as permanent residents or temporary visitors, persons
in port but on ship board being classified to the adjacent area.
In general these definitions apply to the population as enumerated at previous
censuses, and shown in Table 2.
ADMINISTRATIVE
DIVISIONS.
The County is divided into fifteen county districts (eight urban and seven
rural) comprising 141 district electoral divisions, seventeen of which are situate
in urban districts (see Table 3).
Of this latter group thirteen are coterminous
with wards.
Eight poor law unions are contained within the County-five wholly and
three partly; the remaining portions of the divided unions being situate in Armagh
County, Down County, and Belfast County Borough. The n1edical dispensary
districts situate i11 the County number thirty-eight (see Table 4).
Poor law
unions and medical dispensary districts are, respectively, superintendent registrars'
and registrars' districts for the registration of marriages, births, and deaths.
The County is also divided into twenty-three county electoral divisions (see
Table 5) ; and into five regional education areas (see Table 12).
Apart from the reconstitution of county electoral divisions and district
electoral divisions in 1923, the changes which have taken place in the boundaries
of administrative divisions since 1911 are as follows:-
(a) Portion of Londonderry County transferred to Antrin1 County consequent
on the extension of Portrush Urban District (1st April, 1915).
(b) Portion of Ballymoney Rural District transferred to Portrush Urban
District (1st April, 1915).
(c) Ballymoney Poor._ Law Union enlarge~d by the transfer to Portrush Medical
Dispensary District of the portion of Londonderry County referred to
at (a).
(lst April, 1915).


---

## Page 7

•..
Vll.
(d) Aghalee Rural District abolished and its area added to Lis burn Rural
District (1st April, 1920).
(e) Ballycastle Urban District fanned out of portion of Ballycastle Rural
District, and divided into two wards (1st April, 1921, and 6th November,
1922, respectively).
(f) Lisburn Urban District divided into three wards (9th November, 1922).
(g) VVhitehead (Larne R.D .) constituted a Town under the Towns Improve-
ment (I.) Act, 1854, and certain consequential changes 1nade in the D.E.D.'s
of Island Magee South, Templecorran, and \Nhitehead (1st April and 24th
June, 1925, respectively.)
PARLIAMENTARY CONSTITUENCY.
The present parliamentary constituency was created by the Government
of Ireland Act, 1920, and comprises the administrative county of Antrim.
HOUSES.
1. The term "House" denotes
(a) each structure (except institutions) which stands alone or is separated
fron1. another by a vertical party wall ; and which is used or intended
partly or wholly for habitation ;
(b) each institution (viz., hospital, sanatorium, nursing home, infirmary,
vvorkhouse, asylum, religious or charitable community, residential
school or college, barracks or other premises under Naval, l\1ilitary,
Air Force, or Police discipline, prison or other place of detention),
regardless of the number of buildings within its precincts ;
(c) each vessel, caravan, tent, or other movable dwelling.
The 1911 figures for "I-Iouses" do not include vessels, and each separate
building within the precincts of an institution was at that Census regarded as a
separate " Ifouse."
2.
The terms "Boarding-House" and "Lodging-House" denote, in addition
to habitations so described, any private house containing three or more boarders
or lodgers.
3.
The term " Private House " denotes all habitations other than
(a) dwellings within the precincts of an institution;
(b) hotels, boarding houses, and other such communal dwellings;
(c)
premises in which three or more boarders, lodgers, or trade assistants
reside;
(d) residences occupied by virtue of the tenant's employment, (except
rectories and manses, and dwellings belonging to · but detached
from domestic establishments) ;
(e) premises occupied by caretakers;
(f)
ships, caravans, tents, and other movable dwellings ;
(g) barns, stables, and out-houses occupied on Census night.
The term includes dwellings over shops except when these are occupied as
described at (c) and (d) above.
4.
The term " Inhabited " denotes actual residence of a person or persons
on Census night.
" Uninhabited " houses, therefore, include those temporarily
vacant through the absence of the usual occupiers as well as those without tenants.
Enu1nerators were instructed to omit from their enumeTation empty houses in
such an advanced state of decay that there was little likelihood ~hat they woul.d
again be made fit for habitation. In this matter they were required to use the1r
own judgment.


---

## Page 8

.
~.,·e'il
Vlll.
ROOMS.
I. The rooms enumerated are the usual living tooms, 1ncluding beclroo1ns
and kitchens, but excluding sculleries (i .. e., rooms used only for washing, cleaning,
or cooking), pantries, cloakrooms, landings, closets or bathroo1ns, or any ware-
house, office or shop rooms.
2.
For the purpose of classifying. fami~ies by rooms occupied .(~able 8),
shared-room occupations-i.e., those 111 which two . or 1nore families have
rooms in common,-have been rounded up to the next Integral nun1ber of rooms.
Thus, three families occupying 1-§-, 2~, and 2~ rooms in a 6-roomed house would
be shown as occupying 2, 3, and 3 roon1s respectively. The extent of the rounding-
up process may be ascertained in a general way by reference to Table 7, in which
the actual number of rooms is given.
Generally, in cases of 1 rootn occupations,
all persons living therein, whether related or not, have been enumerated as one
Census family.
FAMILIES.
1. The term " Family " denotes
(a) The inhabitants of each institution (see 1 (b) of" Houses " definition),
hotel, boarding-house, lodging-house, or ship.
(b) Each person or group of persons occupying separately the whole, or part
of, any dwelling other than as described at (a).
In 1911 the inhabitants of premises described at (a) above were in certain
cases enumerated as two or more families.
2.
" Private Families" mean all families enumerated in " Private Houses "
(see 3 of '' Houses " definition).
RATEABLE
VALUATION.
The figures shown are those for service of the year ended 31st March, 1927,
and have been supplied by the Commissioner of Valuation.
Rateable heredita--
ments include lands, buildings, tolls, railways, tram-roads, canals, fisheries,
turbaries, mines, quarries, waterworks, telegraphs, and telephones. The figures
throughout exclude the valuation of electric, gas, and water mains, telegraphs,
telephones, fi.sheTies and (in certain cases) railways and waterworks, the amounts
concerned being aggregated and shown for the county, county districts, and
poor law unions in foot-notes to the appropriate tables.
EDUCATION.
'rhe classification under educational establishments (which was assisted
by reference to _lists supplied for Census purposes by the Ministry of Education)
is as follows:----
I. PUBLIC ELEMENTARY ScHoots.
Day and evening pupils are grouped
together in this class, the question on the Census schedule not
requiring a separation as between day and evening in respect of
pupils attending public elementary schools.
2.
SECONDARY ScHOOLS, including preparatory departments and preparatory
schools.
This heading relates to day pupils only, and the schools
are limited to those recognised by the Ministry of Education.
3.
TECHNlCAL ScHOOLS. The day pupils shown in this group comprise those
stated to be attending junior technical schools, day commercial
classes} and day classes for domestic economy and manual instruction
which were being held at certain centres in April, 1926. The evening
pupils comprise those stated to be attending technical schools or
classes in the evening.


---

bt.
4.
OTHER EDUCATIONAL EsTABLISHMENTS (Day and Evening).
These in-
clude secondary schools not recognised by the Ministry of Education,
universities, training colleges for teachers, agricultural schools,
commercial schools, home or private tuition, ·etc.
OCCUPATIONS.
The occupational classification is based on that used in the Reports on the
Census of England and Wales, 1921. *
It will be seen that t11ere are thirty-tvvo orders.
Each of these covers the
sa1ne occupational ground as the England a11d \Vales orders.
Eleven of the
orders are divided into thirty-two sub-orders, each of which is con1parable with
the corresponding England and \Vales sub-order, with the following exceptions:-

INDUSTRIES.
The industrial classification is based on that used in the Reports on the
Census of England and Wales, I92l. *
It will be seen that there are twenty-two orders. Each of these covers the
same industrial ground as the England and Wales orders.
Nine of the orders
are divided into thirty-eight sub-orders, each of which is comparable with the
corresponding England and Wales sub-order, with the following exceptions:-
Northern Ireland

The industry table contained in this volume shows figures for orders and

BIRTHPLACES.
Persons stated to have been born in Ireland-the County name or
"Irish Free State" not being inserted-are assumed to have been born in the
Irish Free State. Where nationality is given but birthplace is not, it has been
assumed that the person was born in the country of vvhich he or she is a national.
All persons of British nationality and unstated birthplace have been regarded
as born in the County of enumeration.
RELIGIOUS
PROFESSIONS.
The religious professions, other than Roman Catholic, Presbyterian, Church
of Ireland, and Methodist, have been classified under the headings set out at foot
of Table 18, separate mention being made of all professions containing IO or more

## Page 10

x.
adherents. The heading "Presbyterian" includes those who .call themselves
by this name without qualification, together with those returned as me1nbers of
the Established Church of Scotland.
Other Presbyterian deno1nina tions are
shown at the foot of tP.e table.
lVIembers of the Church of England and the
Episcopal Church of Scotland have not been included under Church of Ireland
except where stated. The latter group includes all persons returned as "Church"
or " Episcopalian."
ORPHANHOOD.
In cotnpiling statistics under this head, step-parents, foster-parents, and
adoptive parents, so stated, have been treated in the same 1nanner as natural
parents. Where the information as to one parent is stated as "not known,'~
that parent has been assumed to be dead and the case dealt with accordingly.
Where information as to neither parent is given, the case has been placed in the
" No Statement " group.
INFIRMITIES.
I. DEAF AND DuMB, DuMB, BLIND.
The figures given are in respect of
persons so returned, except \vhere the answers indicate only partial infinnity as
"nearly blind."
2.
CRIPPLED.
In addition to those persons specifically described as
"Crippled," the figures given include all cases in which it is clearly indicated
that the person
(a) has suffered a total permanent loss of the use of arm, hand, leg, or
foot;
(b) is permanently crippled in such a degree as to prevent the free
pursuit of most ordinary occupations.
The following cases, treated as "Crippled," indicate the variety of the terms
used in answering the question on the Census schedule:-
Arm-deformed, disabled ; hand-deformed ; ·hip joint disease ; knee-
decayed, tubercular arthritis in ; leg-disabled, diseased bone in,
infirm, short ; on crutch ; paralysis-infantile, nervous, of arms and
side, of legs and back, of spine ; rheumatoid arthritis ; spinal caries ;
unable to walk.
3.
MENTALLY AFFLICTED.
All cases returned, whatever the degree of
affliction, are included in the tables. The following terms were used in the
returns:-
Afflicted; feeple minded; imbecile; melancholia; 1nental debility; simple;
weak intellect.
4.
Persons suffering from more than one infirmity have been classiiied to
that which appears most likely to constitute the greater, or greatest, disability,
infirmities being graded for this purpose from greatest to least, thus:-
Mentally afflicted;
blind;
crippled; deaf and dumb; dumb.
SPELLING OF PLACE NAMES.
The spelling of place names follows that in the 1911 Census volumes,
except that corrections have been made to secure agreement with the spelling on the
Ordnance Survey maps where differences were revealed in the course of the work.
The spelling now may be regarded as generally following that adopted by the
Ordnance Survey Department.


---

## Page 11

xi.
CONVENTION
AS
TO
DOTS (.)
AND
DASHES (-).
Throughout this volume dots(.) are used in tabular matter to indicate nil (0),
and dashes ( -) are used to indicate that information cannot or is not intended
to be given.
CORRECTION
OF
CENSUS DATA.
Every effort has been made to eliminate, as far as practicable, errors and
inaccuracies from the Census data. The process of correction began with the
Enumerators, who received full instructions as to the action to be taken in cases
of error or omission; it was continued by a system of check applied at each
stage of the office work ; and was completed by an intensive scrutiny and inter-
comparison of the tabulation sheets.
This scrutiny and comparison revealed
a certain number of incongruities, which, when material, were investigated and
· corrected in the light of the facts disclosed.
Errors of a minor nature (i.e.,
such as could not affect the statistical value of the Census tables) were adjusted
more or less arbitrarily.

## Page 12

xii.
PART II.
STATISTICAL NOTES.
The definitions etc. contained in Part I. of this volume apply with equal
force to the content~ of th~se notes and to the tables contained in Part I~I. , Roman
numerals are used in this volume for the tables in Part II, and Arable numerals
for the tables in Part III.
AREA.
The area of Antrim County, exclusive of the larger rivers, lakes, and tide-ways,

    is 1,098 square miles or 21 per cent. of the area of Northern Ireland. In 1~96 the
County lost almost nine square n1iles to Belfast C?unty Borough, but ga1ned at
later ·dates an aggregate of about half a square nule, partly from Down County
and partly from Londonderry County (see note to Table 2).
POPULATION.
The population numbers 191,643 persons, having declined slightly since 19~1 ;
a continuation of the downward movement recorded at each Census enun1erat1on
from 1851 onwards.
. The actual decrease, after adjustn1ent on account of the
transfer of portion of Londonderry County, amounts to 2,490 persons, representing
an average annual decrease of less than 0.1 per cent.

(a) The populations given relate to the County as constituted at the beginning of the intercensal period, and the
births and deaths to the County as constituted at the time of thdr registration. A portion of the County
of Londonderry was transferred to the County of Antrim in 1915, consequent on the extension o-£ Portrush
Urban District.
Owing to the fact that prior to 1899, certain registration units in Belfast Union extended
over both the urban and rural portions thereof, it is not practicable to show :figures in this table for periods
prior to 1901.
{b) Including the deaths of non-civilians of the County of Antrim which occurred outside Northern Ireland. It has
not been found possible to estimate the number of these deaths at all closely, but from the War mortality lists
of soldiers from Ireland, which were compiled by the War Office from regimental records, it a1)pears that for
the whole of Northern Ireland the non-civilian deaths which occurred outside the country numbered about
10,000 during the last intercensal period, exclusive of the deaths of regular soldiers stationed outside the country
at the date of the 1911 Census.
While comparable figures of excess of births over deaths are not available
for periods prior to 1901 (see foot-note (a) to Table I), it is evident that the actual
net outward movement during the period 1841-1901 was much greater than the
recorded decrease in population. Statistics as to the size and direction of the
emigration stream are lacking, but there can be no doubt that the rapid grovvth
of Belfast has been materially assisted by population gains from the County.
From Table II it will be seen that urban districts as a whole have increased
in population during the past 15 years, while rural districts as a whole have
decreased-the trend in each aggregate following that exhibited in the preceding
inter-censal period, though the rate of variation is less.
The 1926 urban population

    constitutes 26.2 per cent. of the whole, as against 23.2 per cent. in 1911, and 19.4
per cent. in 190l-the percenta~es quoted relatin~ to urban ar~as ~s con$titutec;l

xiii.
at the date of each census. It is interesting to note that among the urban districts
the greatest relative increases occur in the seaside resorts of Portrush, Larne, and
Ball ycastle ; and that the three rural districts which show increases are all
adjacent to) or within easy reach of, Belfast.

·
The persons per acre rates vary in the urban districts between 32.1 in Carrick-
fergus and 1.9 in Ballyclare.
In rural districts the rates vary between 0.6 and 0.1
persons per acre.
Such rates, however, should be used with rese;rve in measuring
the degree of population density in urban areas, since the acreages of these may
include widely differing proportions of waste land, land under pasture, etc.
Tables 3, 4, and 5 provide more detailed information regarding the population
in the various sub-divisions of the County, and incorporate figures of rateable
valuation. The rateable valuation has increased from £741,555 to £774,881 over
the past fifteen years, an increase of 4. 7 per cent. as compared with a decrease of
1.3 per cent. in population (These figures refer to the ·county as constituted at
the date of the 1926 Census).

l.n

In general, .a man IS q~ahfied If ~1 y~ars of age or upw~rds,

It may be noted that a person may be registered as a parlian1entary elector
in a constituency although not resident therein.
The question of the comparison of the nu1nber of parlia1nentary electors

* The figures given relate to the County as constituted at the date of each Census. For particulars of territorial
changes see note at head of Table 2 (page 1).
While the total number of houses in the County decreased by 1.6 per cent.,
the number of inhabited houses increased by 1.1 per cent.-this being due to a
considerable reduction in the number of uninhabited houses, the 1926 figure for
which is the smallest recorded since 1871.
The increase in inhabited houses, set
against the population decrease of 1.1 per cent., evidences a change in housing
conditions to which further reference is made in the comment on Table V below.
The number of houses under construction is the highest on record, being almost
treble that in 1911.
Persons per Inhabited House and Persons per Family.-The average number
of persons per inhabited house, which showed a steady decrease from 1851 to 1911,
has again shown a decrease fro1n 1911 to 1926. The average number of persons per
family has shown a corresponding decline. In this connection it should be noted that
by u:family" is meant Census family, which, it will be seen from the definition
on page viii, differs from the natural family of parents and children.
~ith regard to th~ habitation of houses by more than one family, there has
?een ~Ittle change dunng the past ha1f-c~ntury, the number of families per 100·

(a) The figures given relate to each county district as constituted at the date of the 1926 Census.
Housing in County Districts.-Table V. shovvs that in ten of the county districts
there has been an increase in the total number of houses and in five a decrease.
Comparison of this Table with Table II. also shows that since 1911 the proportion
of inhabited houses to population has increased in all county districts but
Ballycastle Urban and Portrush Urban, vvhere the process has been reversed.
The large increase in the number of houses under construction in the Rural
Districts of Belfast, Larne and Lisburn-all contiguous to Belfast County Borough
-will be noted.
The extent of sub-letting in the various county districts is best indicated by
the factor "number of private ·families per inhabited private house," which js
readily obtainable from the figures of house accommodation contained in Table 7.
It ¥.rill be seen that the number of families per 100 private houses is 101 for the
County, and that the rate varies between 106 for Belfast Rural District and 100
for several other districts. 97 per cent. of the private families in the County occupy
a whole house each.
Of all the inhabited private houses in the County 99 per cent. are occupied
by single families and 39 per cent. contain 4 to 5 rooms.
Accommodation of Families and Persons.-The occupied room accommodation
in the County approximates to one room per person, the variation from this
average ranging between 1.64 in Portrush Urban District, and 0.92 in Ballycastle
Rural District. Generally speaking, in the urban districts room accommodation
is above the mean; in rural districts belo·w it.
A detailed analysis of the room
accommodation, coupled with the size of family, etc., appears in Table 8.
Over
25 per cent. of the families in the· County are seen to occupy 4 rooms each, the
family of three occurs most frequently, and the most common combination consists
of families of 3, each family occupying 4 rooms.
Table VII. in conjunction with Table 8, will enable comparisons to be made
between the density of occupation in each county district. In these tables the
density of 2 persons and less per roo1n has not been sub-divided, but this is not
intended to imply any judgme,nt as to \vhat in fact constitutes adequate accom-
rpodation, since this matter obviously could not be determined without a k11owled~e


---

xvi.
of other factors such as size of rooms, sanitary and water provision, etc. Sub-
divisions other than those set out in summary form can readily be calculated from
the details given in the :first nine columns of Table 8.

In the County it will be seen that 18 per cent. of the private family population
occupy premises at a density of more than 2 persons per room. The average
density of occupation in urban districts as a whole is little less than that in rural
districts as a whole ; but the distribution of the population over the various
densities differs considerably for the respective aggregates, the proportion of the
private family population occupying premises at a density of more than 2 persons
per room being 14 per cent. in urban districts as against 20 per cent. in rural
districts. The greatest density of occupation is found in Ballycastle Rural District,
where 26 per cent. of the private family population are housed at a density of more
than 2 persons per room.
INSTITUTIONS, · ETC.
The population in each of the types of inhabited buildings, etc., other than
private houses, is shown in Tables 9 and 10, together with the number of inmates
in respect of institutions. The institutions are not individually identified as in
1911, but the number of institutions in each category is given. The inmates of

    the Poor Law Institutions number 122, as against 932 at the date of the 1911
Census. With respect to this comparison it should be noted that pauper patients
are now accommodated in the district hospitals which have replaced workhou.se

    infirmaries in the County. Persons on shipboard number 38 as against 131 in 1911.
"Other premises" include police and military barracks, residential schools,
premises occupied by caretakers, etc.
SEXES,
AGES,
AND
MARITAL CONDITIONS.
Table 11 shows the distribution of the population of the County by
s·ex, age and marital condition.
The following tables summarise the position in
1926 and the changes in the last intercensal period.

Excess of Females over Males.-Table 2 shows that the number of females

    per 1,000 n1ales increased from 1,067 in 1831 to 1,100 in 1871, and has since declined
to 1,070.
More boys are born than girls, but as Table ll shows, the excess of males
over females gradually decreases with increase in age, until frorn about 20 onwards
the females are the more numerous.
Distribution according to age.-The decrease of 1.1 per cent. in the total

    populationis the net result of decreases in age groups 0-19, 30-44, 70-84, and 100
    and over, and increases in age groups 20-29, 45-69, and 85-99.
l\1aterials for an
analysis of the manner in which variations in the numbers of births and deaths
fron1 year to year, the number of war deaths, and the 1novement of population
have combined to produce these changes in age distribution are not available:
the effect has been to raise the average age of the population from approximately

    30 years in 1911 to 30.6 years at the present time.

## Page 18

xviii.
Distribution by Marital Condition.-Table XII shows the percentage variations
in the male and female populations of each marital condition since 1911. The
decrease in the numl:::er of single and vvido-vved persons is proportionately greater
than that in the population as a whole, while the number of married persons has
actually increased.
EDUCATION.
From Table 12 it will be seen that of the total population in Antrim County
19.9 per cent. of the males and 18.1 per cent. of the fe1nales are attending
educational establishments.
Of the children aged 6 to 13 inclusive, 2.8 per cent. of boys and 2.9 per cent.
of girls are not stated to be attending school.
At ages 15 to 19 inclusive, the persons still attending various kinds of educa-
tional establishments represent 13.2 per cent. of the males and 14.5 per cent. of
the females.
The percentages given above are reflected generally in each of the five regional
education areas into which the County is divided.
In endeavouring to draw conclusions fron1 this table it will be necessary to
treat the Census figures relating to persons attending technical schools with con-
siderable reserve, pending further investigation, since a n1arked cli:fference has
been found to exist between them and figures of pupils enrolled in technical schools
as published by the Ministry of Education. F'urther reference to this n1atter will
be made in the General Report on the Census relating to Northern Ireland as a
whole.
OCCUPATIONS.
Proportion Occupied.-From Table XIII it will be seen that both for males
and females the percentage of the total population occupied is higher than in
1911.
In 1926, 43.2 per cent. of the population of both sexes and all ages were
occupied, and thus normally were wage earners. It should be noted that both
employed and out-of-work persons are included in the term "occupied." The
proportion of males aged 12 years and over who are occupied is only a little higher
than in 1911, but the proportion of fP.males of those ages occupied has appreciably
increased.

* Only orders or sub-orders showing proportions of 5 or more per 1,000 occupied males or females are included, and the Single or Grouped
Occupations have been limited to those showing proportions of 10 or more per 1,000.
From Table XV it will be seen that about 43 per cent. of the occupied males
appear under the main group or order II, Agricultural Occupations. This order
contains more than four times as many persons as the next in point of size-XII,
Textile Workers.
The occupations of most numerical importance among the
males are: Farmers; Agricultural Labourers; and Fanners' Relatives assisting
1n the work of the farm.
Of the occupied females 80 per cent. are included under the orders XII,
Textile Workers;
XXVII, Personal Service ; II, Agricultural Occupations;
and XIII, Makers of Textile Goods and Articles of Dress) the most important
occupations numerically being Domestic Service;
Weavers; and Farmers'
Relatives assisting in the work of the farm.
Table 13 shows by sex the number of persons included under each heading
of the full occupational classification.

Of all the occupied population 46.3 per cent. of the males and 62.5 per cent.
of the females are returned as being entitled to benefits under the Nationall-Iealth
Insurance Acts.
For employers and workers on own account of all ages, taken
together, the percentages are 1.8 per cent. of the n1ales and 1.4 per cent. of the
females, and for en1ployees, apprentices and out of work, taken together, they are
62.8 per cent. of the males and 71.0 per cent. of the females.
Of the unoccupied, including retired, 3.0 per cent. of the males and 1.4 per
cent. of the females are returned as entitled to benefits.

!he M~nu_facturing Industries (using that te~m in a wi.de sense) directly
provide a hvehhood for 35 per cent. of the occupied population; Fishing and
Agriculture for 33 per cent. ; and Commerce and Finance (which includes the
wholesale and retail distributive trades, banking, and insurance) for 10 per cent.
The main manufacturing industry is that of Textiles, which provides for 21 per
cent. of the occupied population.
The males outnumber the females in all the groups, except the Professions,
where the females are slightly in excess, and Personal Service, vvhere the females
are almost three times as numerous as the males.
Table 16 shows the industrial distribution of the occupied population in greater
detail, figures being given for each industrial order and sub-order.
BIRTHPLACES.
Table 17 shows, by sex, the numbers of persons born in each of the counties
of Northern Ireland, other countries in the British Isles, overseas Dominions,
foreign countries, and at sea.
Of the total population of Antrim County, 82 per
cent. were born in the County, 5 per cent. in the Belfast County Borough, and
7 per cent. in the other five counties of Northen1 Ireland. Only 0.49 per cent.
were born outside the British Isles, and nearly one half of these vvere born in the
British Dominions and Colonies.
Persons born in foreign countries numbered only
519, and 10 persons -vvere born at sea.

From Table XVIii it will be seen that the proportion of the population
born in Ireland, which now stands at 96.4 per cent. has decreased slightly from
census to census since 1861, while the proportions for the other parts of the British
Isles and abroad each show a gradual increase,

## Page 22

xxii.
RELIGIOUS
PROFESSIONS.
It will be seen from Table XIX that the increase recorded at the 1911 Census
enumeration in the numbers of Protestant Episcopalians and Methodists has
continued during the past 15 years, while the numbers of Roman Catholics, Presby-
terians and Other and Not Stated Denominations, have continued to decrease.
The increase amongst Methodists is both absolutely and relatively the greater. ·
The greatest absolute decrease occurred amongst P~esbyterians (by far the largest
Denomination in the County), and the greatest relative decrease amongst the Other
and Not Stated Denominations.

ORPHANHOOD.
Table 19 shows that of the 59,686 children enumerated in the County at ages
under 16, 6,408 have lost one or both parents. This figure excludes cases of
orphaned children whose loss has been supplied by step-parents or adoptive parents,
and 313 cases in which no statement as to parents was secured.
Of the 6,408
orphans, 57 per cent. are fatherless and 36 per cent. motherless, the remaining 7
per cent. being orphaned by the death of both parents. The high proportion of
fatherless to motherless orphans is in part a corollary of the high proportion of.
widows to widowers, though to some extent it may be due to a possible tendency
to regard the fathers of illegitimate children as dead if their whereabouts are
unknown.
INFIRMITIES.
The number of persons returned as both Deaf and Dumb is 69, or 4 7 less than ..
the number returned in 1911.
The Dumb Only numbered 20, which compares
with 37 in 1911.

    The number returned as Blind has increased from 161 in 1911 to 202 in 1926.
The total number of persons who have been classified as Crippled is 841,
comprising 514 males and 327 females.
It has not been found possible to test
the reliability of this figure, and it should be accepted with due reserve, both on
account of the broad interpretation which necessarily had to be placed on the
term "crippled" for classification purposes, and because of a possible reluctance
on the part of some crippled persons to disclose their infirmity by stating it on
the census schedule.
Of the 705 persons returned as mentally afflicted, 515 -vvere enumerated in
the District Lunatic Asylum, and 10 in other institutions. In 1911 the persons
returned as Lunatic or Idiot numbered 871.
Of these 582 were enumerated in
~he D~strict Lunatic Asylum and 82 in Ballymena Workhouse-the latter group
1nclud1ng 53 persons stated to have been "on the books of the Belfast District
Lunatic Asylum."
Particulars of the sex, age, and marital condition of persons suffering from
infirmities will be found in Table 20.

The remainder of the town is in Carnmoney (105) D.E.D. in
this Rural District. The entire town contains 250 persons.
(b) The town of Longlands or Coggrey stands on the townland of :Sallygolan, in which its area, etc., are included.
This town was erroneously 'Ieturned in 1911 as standing on
Whitehouse townland. The 1011 population and house figures for Whitehouse townland luwe been adjusted accordingly.

DEFINITIONS. -1. Definitions of the terms "Area," "Population," "Houses," "Inhabited,'' "Private Families," "Rooms," and "Rateable
Valuation " will be found on pages vi to viii.

    2. The 1911 :fiaures tlu·o11ghout are for the County aml its admini.stmtive divisions as constituted at the da,te of the 1926 Census.
(See note g'n Administrative Divisions on page vi.)
2. Dots (.} indicate nil (0), and dashes (-) indicate that information cannot or is not intended to be given.
ARRANGEl\:IENT.-1. Poor Law Unions are in alphabetical order, and, under each of these, Dispensary Districts are arranged in a similar order.
2. District Electoral Divisions are arranged in alphabetical order under each Dispensary District.
3. The reference in the second column of the Table is to the serial number prefixed to the name of each District Electoral Division
in tho first column of Table 3.

## Page 100

iii.
PREFACE.
This volume has been prepared in accordance with the prov1s1ons of
Section 6 (I) of the Census Act (Northern Ireland), 1925.
The 1926 Census
statistics which it contains were compiled from the returns made as at midnight
of the l8-19th April, 1926: they supersede those in the Preliminary Report
published in August, 1926, and may be regarded as final.
The Census publications will consist of:-
1.
SEVEN CouNTY VoLUMES, each similar in design and scope to the present
publication.
2. A GENERAL REPORT relating to Northern Ireland as a whole, covering
in more detail the statistics shown in the County Volumes, and
containing in addition tables showing
(i.) the occupational distribution of persons engaged in each of
51 groups of industries ;
(ii.) the distribution of the foreign born population by nationality,
age, marital condition, and occupation ;
(iii.) the distrib-p.tion of families of dependent children under 16
years of age, by age, sex, marital condition, and occupation of
parent;
(iv.) the occupational distribution of persons suffering from infirmities.
3.
A TOPOGRAPHICAL INDEX, dealing in separate alphabetical sections with
each type of area for which statistics are published in the County
series. The particulars given for each territorial unit include references
to the 6" Ordnance Survey maps and to Tables 3, 4, or 5 of the
relative County Volume.
The net prices for the above publications will be 5s. Od. for each County
Volume and lOs. Od. each for the General Report and the Topographical Index.
A complete set of these volumes may be obtained. directly
from
the
Superintendent, H.M. Stationery Office, 15, Donegall Square "'est, Belfast, at the
special inclusive price of £2 2s. Od. strictly net.
·The Registrar-General is empowered by Section 6 (2) of the Census Act
(Northern Ireland), 1925, to prepare and supply from the Census returns, statistics
other than those published in the Census Reports at the request and cost of any
local authority or person. Any special demands for statistics more detailed than
those published (either as regards subject matter or area) can thus, as far as office
records permit, be satisfied.
Requests for special compilation of Census statistics
should be addressed to the Registrar-General, who will be pleased to advise and
furnish an estimate of cost.
-
GENERAL REGISTER OFFICE,
MuRRAY STREET,
BELFAST.

## Page 103

Vl.
PART I.
DEFINITIONS AND
TERMS USED IN
EXPLANATIONS OF
THIS VOLUME.
AREA.
The acreages given are as measured by the Ordnance Survey of 1899-l 907.
They exclude-
In Tables I, 3-5 (a) fore-shore and unnan1ed islands of less than one rood
in extent.
In Tables 3-5
(b) Carlingford and Strang ford Loughs.
(c) the larger rivers, lakes, and tideways.
N .B.-While the larger rivers, lakes, and tideways are not enclosed within
townland boundaries, they forn1 part of ad1ninistrative areas. The extent of
these excluded portions is shown for the county, county districts, and poor law
unions in foot-notes to the appropriate tables.
POPULATION.
The population comprises all persons, including Forces of the Crown, enumer-

    ated as at midnight of Sunday the 18th-19th April, 1926, together with those who,
not having been enumerated elsewhere, arrived in port or were received into any
dwelling on the n1orning of the 19th April before the Census returns were collected.
The population assigned to each area is that actually present (as defined
above) within it, whether as permanent residents or temporary visitors, persons
in port but on ship board being classified to the adjacent area.
In general these definitions apply to the population as enumerated at previous
censuses, and shown in Table 2.
ADMINISTRATIVE
DIVISIONS.
The County is divided into eighteen county districts (ten urban and eight
rural), comprising 146 district electoral divisions, twenty-two of which are situate
in urban districts (see Table 3).
Of this latter group seventeen are cotern1inous
with wards bearing the· same name, and one is divided into t\vo wards.
Eight poor law unions are contained within the county-three wholly and five
partly; the remaining portions of the divided unions being situate in Antrim
County, Armagh County, and Belfast County Borough.
The medical dispensary
districts situate in the County number thirty-six (see Table 4).
Poor law unions
and medical dispensary districts are, respectively, superintendent registrars' and
registrars' districts for the registration of marriages, births, and deaths.
The County is also divided into twenty county electoral divisions (see Table
5) ; and forms a single regional education area (see Table 12).
Apart from the reconstitution of county electoral divisions and district electoral
divisions in 1923, the changes ,;vhich have taken place in the boundaries of adminis-
trative divisions since 1911 ·are as follows:-
(a) Newcastle Urban District divided into two wards (6th Noven1ber, 1922).
(b) Warrenpoint Urban District divided into two wards (9th November, 1922).
(c) Wards in Newry Urban District reconstituted (13th November~ 1922).


---

## Page 104

...
Vll.
(d) Portion of Downpatrick Rural District and Union transferred to New-
townards Rural District and Union (1st April, 1924.)
(e) Portion of N ewto\vnards Rural District transferred to Bangor Urban
District (1st June, 1924).
(f) Wards and District Electoral Divisions in Bangor Urban District altered
consequent on the extension of the Urban District (1st and 20th ] une,
1924, respectively).
(g) Downpatrick, formerly a town under the Towns Improvement (I.) Act,
1854, and forming part of Downpatrick Rural District, created an
urban district (1st April, 1925) .
. PARLIAMENTARY
CONSTITUENCY.
The present parliamentary constituency vvas created bjl the Government
of Ireland Act, 1920, and comprises the administrative county of Down.
HOUSES.
l. The term " House " denotes
(a) each structure (except institutions) which stands alone or is separated
fron1 another by a vertical party wall; and which is used or intended
partly or wholly for habitation;
(b) each institution (viz., hospital, sanatorium, nursing home, infirmary,
workhouse, asylum, religious or charitable community, residential
school or college, barracks or other premises under Naval, lVIilitary,
Air Force, or Police discipline, prison or other place of detention),
regardless of the number of buildings within its precincts ;
(c) each vessel, caravan, tent, or other movable dwelling.
The 1911 figures for "Houses" do not include vessels, and each separate
building within the precincts of an institution was at that Census regarded as a
separate " I-!ouse."
2.
The terms" Boarding-House" and" Lodging-House" denote., in addition
to habitations so described, any private house containing three or more boarders
or lodgers.
:3.
The term "Private House" denotes all habitations other than
(a)
dwellings within the precincts of an institution ;
(b)
hotels, boarding-houses, and other such communal dwellings ;
(c)
(d)
(e)
(f)
(g)
premises in which three or more boarders, lodgers, or trade assistants
reside;
residences occupied by virtue of the tenant's employment, (except
rectories and manses, and dwellings belonging to but detached
from do1nestic establishments) ;
pren1ises occupied by caretakers;
ships, caravans, tents, and other movable dwellings;
barns, stables, and out-houses occupied on Census night.
The term includes dwellings over shops except when these are occupied as
described at (c) and (d) above.
4. The term "Inhabited" denotes actual residence of a person or persons,
on Census night.
"Uninhabited" houses, therefore, include those temporarily
vacant through the absence of the usual occupiers. as well as t1~ose without tenant_s.
Enumerators were instructed to omit from the1r enumeration e1npty houses 1n
such an advanced state of decay that there was little likelihood ~hat they woul.d
·again be made fit for habitation. In this matter they were required to use the1r
own judgn1ent.


---

## Page 105

viii.
ROOMS.
l. The rooms enumerated are the usual living rooms, inclucl~ng bedTo<;n11s
and kitchens, but excluding sculleries (i..e., roon1s used only for washing, cleaning,
or cooking), pantries, cloakrooms, land1ngs, closets or bathroorns, or any ware-
house, office or shop rooms.
2.
For the purpose of classifying: famil.ies by rooms occupied. ~Table 8),
shared-room occupations-i.e., those In which two . or n1ore farn1hes
have
rooms in common,-have been rounded up to the next Integral number of roon1s.
Thus, three families occupying It, 2!, and 2-§- rooms in a 6-roon1ed house ·wc~ulcl
be shown as occupying 2, 3~ and.3 rooms respectively. The exten~,of the rc!undn:g-
up process may be ascertained 1n a general way by reference to I able 7, 111 w:luch
the actual number of rooms is given. Generally, in cases of 1 roon1 occupations,
all persons living therein, whether related or not, have been enun1erated as one
Census family.
FAMILIES.
1. The term "Family" denotes
(a) The inhabitants of each institution (see 1 (b) of " Houses " definition),
hotel, boarding-house, lodging-house, or ship.
(b) Each person or group of persons occupying separately the whole, or
part of, any dwelling other than as described at (a).
In 1911 the inhabitants of pren1ises described at (a) above were in certain
cases enurnerated as two or more families.
2.
"Private Families" mean all families entnnerated in " Private I-Iouses"
(see 3 of '' Houses" definition).
RATEABLE
VALUATION.
The figures shown are those for service of the year ended 31st l\1arch, 1927,
and have been supplied by the Commissioner of Valuation.
Rateable heredita-
ments include lands, buildings, tolls, railways, traru-roads, canals, fisheries,
turbaries, rnines, quarries, waterworks, telegraphs, and telephones.
The figures
throughout exclude the valuation of gas, water~ and electric mains, telegraphs,
telephones, fisheries, rights of taking seaweed, and (in some cases) gasworks and
waterworks; the amounts concerned being aggregated and shown foT the county,
count:y districts, and poor law unions in footnotes to the appropriate tables.
EDUCATION.
The classification under educational establishm.ents (which was assisted
by reference to lists supplied for Census purposes by the Ministry of Education)
is as follows :-
1.
PuBLIC ELEMENTARY ScHOOLS.
Day and evening pupils are grouped
together in this class, the question on the Census schedule not
requiring a separation as between day and evening in respect of
pupils attending public elementary schools.
2.
SECONDARY ScH?OLS, i~cluding preparatory departments and preparatory
scho?ls:
Th1s head1ng relates to day pupils only, and the schools
are hm1ted to those reco'gnised by the Ministry of Education.
3. TECHNICAL ScHOOLS.
The day pupils shown in this group comprise
those stated to be attending junior technical schools, day comn1ercial
cla~ses, and d3:y classes for domestic economy and manual instruction
whi~h were b~rng held at certain centres in April, 1926. The evening
pup1ls compnse those stated to be attending technical schools or
classes in the evening.

universities,
training

agric~ltural schools,
comn1ercial schools, home or private tuition, etc.

INDUSTRIES.
The industrial classification is based on that used 111 the Reports on the

It will be seen that there are twenty-two orders.
Each of these cover the

BIRTHPLACES.
Persons stated .to have been born in Ireland-the County name or "Irish
Free State" not being inserted-are assumed to have been born in the Irish Free
State. Where nationality is given but birthplace is not, it has been assumed that
the person was born in the country of which he or she is a national.
All persons
of British nationality and unstated birthplace have been regarded as borr,;t in the
County of enumeration.
RELIGIOUS
PROFESSIONS.
The religious professions, other than Roman Catholic, Presbyterian, Church
of Ireland, and Methodist, have been classified under the headings set out at foot
of Table I8, separate mention being made of all professions containing 10 or more
* Full details of the English system of classification are published in the "Classification of Occupations,

## Page 107

X.
adherents.
The heading "Presbyterian" includes those who call then1selves
by this narne '~rithout qualification, together -vvi~h those r.eturned as. meJ.?bers of
the Established Church of Scotland.
Other Presbytenan deno1n1nat1ons are
shuwn at the foot of the table.
lVIen1bers of the Church of England and the Epis-
copal Church of Scotland have not been included under Church of Ireland except
where stated. The latter group includes all persons returned as "Church" or
'' Episcopalian.''
ORPHANHOOD.
In con1piling statistics under this head, step-parents, foster-parents, and
adoptive parents, so stated, have been treated in the sarne manner as natural
parents.
\Vhere the information as to one parent is stated as " not known,"
that parent has been assumed to be dead and the case dealt w·ith accordingly.
Where information as to neither parent is given, the case has been placed in the
"No Statement" group.
INFIRMITIES.
1.
DEAF AND DuMB, DUMB, BLIND.
The figures given are in respect of
persons so returned, except where the answers indicate only partial infirn1ity as
"nearly blind."
2.
CRIPPLED.
In addition to those persons specifically described as
"Crippled," the figures given include all cases in which it is clearly indicated
that the person
(a) has suffered a total permanent loss of the use of arm, hand, leg, or
foot;
(b) is permanently crippled in such a degree as to prevent the free
pursuit of most ordinary occupations.
The following cases, treated as "Crippled," indicate the variety of the tenus
used in answering the question ~n1 the Census schedule:-
Arm-deformed, disabled; hand-deformed; hip joint disease; knee-
decayed, tubercular arthritis in; leg-disabled, diseased bone in,
infirm, short ; on crutch ; paralysis-infantile, nervous, of arms and
side, of legs and back, of spine; rheumatoid arthritis; spinal caries;
unable to walk.
3.
MENTALLY AFFLICTED.
All cases returned, whatever the degree of
affliction, are included in the tables.
The following terms were used in the
returns:--
Afflicted; feeble minded; imbecile; melancholia; n1ental debility; simple;
weak intellect.
4.
Persons suffering from more than one infirmity have been classified to
that which appears most likely to constitute the greater, or greatest, disability,
infirmities being graded for this purpose from greatest to least, thus :-
Mentally afflicted ; blind ; crippled ; deaf and dumb ; dumb.
SPELLING
OF
PLACE
NAMES.
The spelling .of place names follows that in the 1911 Census volumes,
except that corrections have been n1ade to secure agreement with the spelling on the
Ordnance Survey maps where differences were revealed in the course of the vvork.
The spelling now rna y be regarded as generally following that adopted by the
Ordnance Survey Department.


---

## Page 108

XL
CONVENTION
AS
TO
DOTS (.)
AND
DASHES (-).
Throughout this volume dots (.) are used in tabular matter to indicate nil (0),
and dashes (-) are used to indicate that information cannot or is not intended
to be given.
CORRECTION
OF
CENSUS
DATA.
Every effort has been made to eliminate, as far as practicable, errors and
inaccuracies from the Census data. The process of correction began \vith the
Enutnerators, who received full instructions as to the action to be taken in cases
of error or on1ission; it was continued by a system of check applied at each
stage of the office ·work; and was completed by an intensive scrutiny and inter-
comparison of the tabulation sheets.
This scrutiny and comparison revealed
a certain number of incongruities, which, ·when material, vvere investigated and
corrected in the light of the facts disclosed.
Errors of a n1inor nature (i.e., such as
could not affect the statistical value of the Census Tables) \:Vere adjusted more or
less ar bi traril y.

## Page 109

xii.
PART II.
STATISTICAL NOTES.
The definitions, etc, contained in Part I of this volume apply with equal
force to the contents of these notes and to the tables contained in Part III. Roman
numerals are used in this volume for the tables in Part II, and Arabic numerals
for the tables in Part III.
AREA.
The area of Down County, exclusive of the larger rivers, lakes, and tideways,

    is 951 square miles, or 18.2 per cent. of the area of Northern Ireland. In 1896 and
1899 the County lost an aggregate of almost five square miles to Belfast County
Borough and Antrim County, but gained in the latter year over half a square mile
from Armagh County (see note to Table 2).
POPULATION.
The population numbers 209,228 persons, or 2.4 per cent. more than in 1911.
The actual increase amounts to 4,925 persons, and is the first recorded since 1841.
(Figures for the earlier periods are shown in Table 2).

(a) Owing to the fact that prior to 1899 certain registration units in Belfast Union extended over both the
urban and rural portions thereof, it is not practicable to show figures in this table for periods prior to 1901.
(b) Including the deaths of non-civilians of the County of Down which occurred outside Northern Ireland.
It has not been found possible to estimate the number of these deaths at all closely, but from the
War mortality lists of soldiers from Ireland, which were compiled by the vVar Office from regimental records, it
appears that for the whole of Northern Ireland the non-civilian deaths which occurred outside the country
numbered about 10,000 during the last intercensal period, exclusive of the deaths of regular soldiers stationed
outside the country at the date of the 1911 Census.
V\lhile comparable figures of excess of births over deaths are not available
for periods prior to 1901 (see foot-note (a) to Table I), it is evident that the actual
net outward movement during the period 1841-1901 was much greater than the
recorded decrease in population. Statistics as to the size and direction of the
emigration stream are lacking, but there can be no doubt that the rapid growth of
Belfast has been assisted to a considerable degree by population gains from the
County.
From Table II it will l:e seen that urtan districts as a whole have increased
in population during the past 15 years, while rural districts as a whole have
~ecreased-the trend in each aggregate following that exhibited in the preceding
1ntercensal p~riod. The 1926 ~rban population constitutes 27.5 per cent. of the

    whole, as ag~1nst 22.9 per cent. 1n 1911, ~nd 19.6 per cent. in 1901-the percentages
quoted relating to urban area$ as constituted at the date of each census,

(a) The figures given are for the County districts as constituted at the date of the 1926 Census.
* Exclusive of Carlingford and Strangford Loughs, the areas of which are not available, and 378 acres under
the larger rivers, lakes, an¢1. tideways.
All urban districts on the coast have increased considerably in population
since 1911, the gain in Bangor-65.3 per cent.-being specially noteworthy.
Castlereagh, adjoining Belfast County Borough, is the only rural district for which
a, considerable increase is recorded.
The persons per acre rates vary in the urban districts between 21.3 in
Newtownards and 1.7 in Newcastle.
Such rates, however, should be used with
reserve in measuring the degree of population density in urban areas, since
the acreages of these may include widely difiering proportions of waste land,
land under pasture, etc. In rural districts the rates vary between 0.6 and 0.2
persons per acre.
Tables 3, 4, and 5 provide more detailed information regarding the population
in the various sub-divisions of the County, and incorporate :figures of rateable
valuation. The rateable valuation has increased fron1 £841,377 to £893,941 over
the past .fifteen years, an increase of 6.2 per cent. as compared with the 2.4 per
cent. increase in population.
Parliamentary Constituency .-In considering the percentages shown in
Table III, regard shot!-ld be given to the qualifications entitling a person to be
registered as an elector. In general, a man is qualified if 21 years of age or upwards,
with the requisite residence or business premises qualification : a woman, if 30
years of age ·or upwards, and if (l) entitled to be registered as a local government
elector in respect of the occupation of land or premises (not being a dwelling-
house) of a yearly value of not less than £5, or of a dwelling-house ; or (2) the wife
of a husband entitled to be so registered,

*The figures given are for the County as constituted at the date of each Census. For particulars of territorial
changes see note to Table 2.
While the total number of houses in the County decreased by 0.8 per cent.,
the number of inhabited houses increased by 2.6 per cent.-this being due to a
considerable reduction in the number of uninhabited houses, the 1926 figure for
which is the smallest Tecorded since 1871. The percentage increase in
inhabited houses, by a figure practically equal to that in population (2.4 per cent.),
indicates that housing conditions in the County as a whole have undergone little
or no change since 1911. Further reference to this matter is made in the comment
on Table V. below.
The nun1ber of houses under construction was the highest recorded since 1831,
and roughly double that in 1911.
Persons per Inhabited House and Persons per Family.-The average number
both of persons per inhabited house and persons per family declined steadily from

    1851 to 1911. A further sljght fall in the latter figure was registered in 1926, but

(a) The figures given are for the County districts as constituted at the date of the 1926 Census.
Housing in County Districts.-Table V shows that there has been a
decrease in the total number of houses in ten county districts, and an increase
in eight county districts. Generally speaking, the decreases occur in urban
districts inland and rural districts remote from Belfast : the increases in urban
districts on the coast and rural districts in the vicinity of Belfast.
Comparison
of this Table with Table II also shows that since 1911 the proportion of inhabited
houses to population has increased in all county districts but Warrenpoint Urban
and Downpatrick, Hillsborough, and Newry No. 1 Rural, where the process
has been reversed. The improvement of housing conditions in Bangor Urban
and Castlereagh Rural is specially noteworthy, in view of the very large population
increases in those districts.
The extent of sub-letting in the county districts is best indicated by the factor
"number of private families per inhabited private house," which is readily obtain-
able from the figures of house accommodation contained in Table 7. It will be
seen that the number of families per 100 houses is 101 for the County, and that
(except in Holywood Urban and Newry Urban) there is little variation from this
average throughout the various county districts.
98 per cent. of the families in
the County occupy a whole house each.
Of all the inhabited private houses in the County 99 per cent. are occupied
by single fan1ilies and 38 per cent. contain 4 to 5 rooms.
Accommodation of Families and Persons.-The occupied room accommoda-
tion in the County approximates to one room per person, the variation from this
average ranging between 1.55 in Bangor Urban District, and 0.91 in Kilkeel and
Newry No. 1 Rural Districts. Generally speaking, .in urban districts room accom-


---

## Page 113

xvi.
m.odation is above the mean, in rural districts below it. A detailed analysis o:£
the room accommodation, coupled with the size of fan1ily, etc., appears in Table 8.
Of the total families in the County, 25 per cent. occupy 4 rooms each, the family
of 2 occurs most frequently, and the most common combination consists of families
of 4, each family occupying 4 rooms.

Table VII, in conjunction with Table 8, will enable comparisons to be made
between the density of occupation in each county district. In these tables the
density of· 2 persons and less per room has not been sub-divided, but this is not
intended to imply any judgment as to what in fact constitutes adequate
accommodation, since this matter obviously could not be determined without a
knowledge of other factors, such as size of rooms, sanitary and water provision,
etc. Sub-divisions other than those set out in suminary form can readily be
calculated from the details given in the first nine columns of Table 8.
In the County ahnost 17 per cent. of the private family population occupy
premises at a density of more than 2 persons per room. The average density of
occupation in urban districts as a whole is appreciably less than that in rural
districts as whole; and the distribution of the urban and rural population over the
various densities exhibits marked differences, the proportion of the private family
population occupying premises at a density of more than 2 persons per room
being 11.2 per cent in the ur"J?an districts as against 19.0 per cent. in the rural
districts. The greatest density of occupation is found in Kilkeel Rural District,
· where 25.9 per cent. of the private family population are housed at a density of
more than 2 persons per room.
INSTITUTIONS, ETC.
The population in each of the types of inhabited buildings, etc., other than
private houses, is shown in Tables 9 and 10, together with the number of inmates
in respect of institutions. The institutions are not individually identified as in
1911, but the number of institutions in each category is given. The inmates of

    Poor Law Institutions number 332 as against 557 at the date of the 1911 Census.
    ~ersons on_ shipboard. ;number 93 as aga~nst ~43 in 1911.

t Including Divorced.
Excess of Females over Males.-Table 2 shows that the number of females

    per 1,000 males has steadily decreased from 1,127 in 1891 to 1,067 in 1926. More
boys are born than girls, but, as Table 11 shows, the excess of males over females
gradually decreases with increase in age until from about age 25 onwards the
females are the more numerous.
Distribution according to age.-The increase of ~.4 per cent. in the total popu-

    lation is the net result of increases in age groups 0-4, 15-29, 40-69, 80-89, and 95
    and over; and decreases in age groups 5-14, 30-39, 70-79, and 90-94.
Materials
for an analysis of the manner in which variations in the numbers of births and
deaths from year to year, the number of war deaths, and the movement of popula-
tion have combined to produce these changes in age distribution are not available :
the effect has been to raise the average age of the population from approximately

    30.3 years in 1911 to 30.7 years at the present time.
Distribution by Marital Conditioh.-Table XII shows the percentage variation
in the male and female populations of each marital condition since 1911. It will
be noted that the married persons have increased at a greater rate, and the
single males at a lesser rate, than the population as a whole ; while the single
females and widowed persons have actually decreased.
~· Table VIII is given for Belfast and .Londonderry County Boroughs only, and Table IX for Belfast County
Borough only.

From Table 12 it will be seen that of the total population in the County 19.1
per cent. of the males and 17.4 per cent. of the females are attending educational
establishments.
Of the children aged 6 to 13 inclusive, 3.1 per cent. of boys and 3.7 per cent.
of girls are not stated to be attending school.
At ages 15 to 19 inclusive, the persons still attending various kinds of educa-
tional establishments represent 11.8 per cent. of the males and 15.2 per cent. of
the females.
In endeavouring to draw conclusions from this table it will be necessary to
treat the Census figures relating to persons attending technical schools with con-
siderable reserve, pending further investigation, since a marked difference has been
found to exist between them and figures of pupils enrolled in technical schools as
published by the Ministry of Education. Further reference to this 1natter will
be made in the General Report on ·the Census relating to Northern Ireland as a
whole.
OCCUPATIONS.
Proportion Occupied.-From Table XIII it will be seen that both for males
and females the percentage of the total population occupied is a little higher than

    in 1911. In 1926, 44 per cent. of the population of both sexes and all ages were
occupied, and thus normally were wage earners. It should be noted that both
employed and out-of-work persons are included in the term " occupied."
Of the
males aged 12 years and over, the proportion occupied shows a slight decrease;
of the females, a slight increase.

Occupations have been limited to those showing proportions of 10 or more per 1,000.
From Table XV it will be seen that about 41 per cent. of the occupied males
appear under the main group or order II, Agricultural Occupations. This order
contains nearly five times as many persons as the next in point of size-XXIII
Commerce, Finance and Insurance. The Occupations of most numerical import-
ance among the males are : Farmers ; Agricultural Labourers ; and Farmers1
Relatives assisting in the work of the farm.
Of the occupied females almost 68 per cent. are included under the Orders
XXVII, Personal Service; XII, Textile Workers; and XIII, Makers of Textile
Goods and Articles of Dress, the most important occupations numerically bejng
Domestic Servants ; Sewers and Stitchers ; and Farmers.
Table 13 shows the number of each sex included under each heading of the·
full occupational classification.


---

## Page 117

XX.
Occupational Status and National Health Insurance.-A consideration of the
percentages in the follo·wing table, which has been compiled from Table 14, shovYS
that of the occupied males nearly l in 4 are either employers or worker~ on own
account, the proportion for occupied females being 1 in 7.
Of ·the employees,
apprentices, and out of work taken together, those who were returned as out of
work at the date of the Census represent 12 per cent. in the case of males and
8 per cent. in the case of females.

Of all the occupied population 42.3. per cent. of the m~les and 57.6 per cent.
of the females are returned as being entitled to benefits under the National Health
Insurance Acts. For employers and workers on own account of all ages, taken
together, the percentages are 1.9 per cent. of the males and 1.6 per cent. of the
females; and for employees, apprentices, and out of work, taken together, they
are 57.7 per cent. of the males C\nd 67.0 per cent. of the females.
Of the unoccupied, including retired, 3.4 per cent. of the males and 1.5 per
cent. ®f the females are returned as entitled to benefits.

## Page 118

xxi.
Fishing and Agriculture, and the Manufacturing Industries (using that term
in a wide sense), each directly provide a livelihood for 32 per cent. of the occupied
population. Commerce· and Finance (which includes the wholesale and retail
distributive trades, banking, and insurance) provides for 11 per cent.; and
Personal Service (which includes service in hotels, restaurants, etc.) for 10 per
cent. The main manufacturing industry is that of Textiles, which provides for
18 per cent. of the occupied population.
The males outnumber the fe1nales in all the groups, except the Professions,
where the females are slightly in excess, and Personal Service, where the females
are nearly three times as numerous as the males.
Table 16 shows the industrial distribution of the occupied population in greater
detail, separate figures being given for each industrial order and sub-order.
BIRTHPLACES.
Table 17 shows, by sex, the numbers of persons born in each of the counties
of Northern Ireland, other countries in the British Isles, overseas Dominions,
foreign countries, and at sea.
Of the total population of Down County, 76.3 per
cent. were bom within the county, 7.0 per cent. in Belfast County Borough, and 7.4
per cent. in the other five counties of Northern Ireland.
Only 0.65 per ceRt. were
born outside the British Isles, and nearly one half of these were born in British
Dominions and Colonies.
Persons born in foreign countries numbered only 748,
and 13 persons \Vere born at sea.

    t This :figure comprises 46,591 Church of Ireland, 3, 778 Church of England, and 9 Episcopal Church of Scotland.
ORPHANHOOD.
Table 19 shows that, of the 62,867 children enumerated in the County at
ages under 16, 6,692 have lost one or both parents. This figure excludes cases of
orphaned children whose loss has been supplied by step-parents or adoptive parents,
and 293 cases in which no statement as to parents was secured. Of the 6,692
orphans, 56 per cent. are fatherless and 37 per cent. are motherless, the remaining
7 per cent. being orphaned by the death of both parents. The high proportion of
fatherless to motherless orphans is in part a corollary of the high proportion of
widows to widowers, though to some extent it may be due to a possible tendency
to regard the fathers of illegitimate children as dead if their whereabouts are
unknown.
INFIRMITIES.
The number of persons returned as both Deaf and Dumb is 56, or 28 less than
the number returned in 1911. The Dumb Only numbered 20, which compares
with 32 in 1911.

    The number returned as Blind has increased from 148 in 1911 to 208 in 1926.
The total number of persons who have been classified as Crippled is 792,
comprising 488 males and 304 females. It has not been found possible to test
the reliability of this figure, and it should be accepted with due reserve, both on
account of the broad interpretation, which necessarily had to be placed on the
term "crippled" for classification 'purposes, and because of a possible reluctance
on the part of some crippled persons to disclose their infirmity by stating it on
the census schedule.
.
.
Of the 2,085 persons returned as mentally afflicted 1,814 were enumerated in
Asylums and 14 in other institutions. In 1911 the persons returned as Lunatic
or Idiot numbered 1,319, ofwhom 1,151 were enumerated in Asylums.
Particulars of the sex, age, and marital condition of persons suffering from
infirmities will be found in Table 20.

DEFINITIONS. -1. Definitions of the terms "Area," "Population," "Houses," "Inhabited," •: Private Families,"" Rooms," and" Rateable
Valuation" will be found on pages .vi to viii.
2. The 1911 figures throughout are for the (J~l]nty and its administ~ative divisions as constituted at the date of the 1926 Census.
(See note on Administrative Divisions, pa.ge vi).
3. Dots (.)indicate nil (0), and dashes (-)indicate that information cannot or is not intended to be given.
ARRANGEMENT.-!. Poor Law Unions are in alphabetical order, and, under each of these, Dispensary Districts are arranged in a similar order.
2. District Electoral Divisions are arranged in alphabetical order under each Dispensary District.
3. The reference in the second column of the Table is to the serial:l;mruber prefl.x:ed to the name of each District El9ctoral Division
in the first column of Table 3.

DEFINITIONS.-
1. Definitions of the terms ".Area," "Population," "Houses," "Inhabited," "Private Families," "Rooms" and" Rateable
Valuation " will be found on pages vi to viii.

    2. The 19ll :figures throughout are for the County and its administra.tive divisions as constituted at the d11te of the 1926 Census.
(See note on Administrative Divisions, p11ge vi.)
3. Dots (.}indicate nil (0}, and dashes(-} indicate that information cannot or is not intended to be given.
ARRANGEMENT.-!. County Electoral Divisions are in alphabetical order, and, under each of these, District Electoral Divisions are arranged in a
similar order.
2. The reference in the second column of the Table is to the serial number prefixed to the name of each District Electoral Division
in the first column of Table 3.
ABBREVIATIONS.-C.E.D.=County Electoral Division: D.E.D.=District Electoral Division.

## Page 193

iii.
PREFACE.
This volume has been prepared in accordance with the prov1s1ons of
Section 6 (1) of the Census Act (Northern Ireland), 1925.
The 1926 Census
statistics which it contains were compiled from the returns made as at midnight

    of the 18-19th April, 1926 : they supersede those in the Preliminary Report
published in August, 1926, and may be regarded as final.
The Census· publications will consist of:-·
1.
SEVEN CouNTY VoLUMES, each similar in design and scope to the present
publication.
2.
A GENERAL REPORT relating to Northern Ireland as a whole, covering
in more detail the. statistics shown in the County Volumes, and
containing in addition tables showing
(i.) the occupational distribution of persons engaged in each of
51 groups of industries;
(ii.) the distribution of the foreign born population by nationality,
age, marital condition, and occupation;
(iii.) the distribution of families of dependent children under 16
·
years of age, by age, sex, marital condition, and occupation of
parent;
(iv.) the occupational distribution of persons suffering frominfirmities.
3.
A ToPOGRAPHICAL INDEX, dealing in separate alphabetical sections with
each type of area for which statistics are published in the County
series. The particulars given for each territorial unit include references
to the 6" Ordnance Survey maps and to Tables 3, 4, or 5 of the
relative County Volume.
The net prices for the above publications will be 5s. Od. for each County
Volume and lOs. Od. each for the General Report and the Topographical Index.
A complete set of these volumes may be obtained directly from the
. Superintendent, H.M. Stationery Office, 15, Donegan Square West, Belfast, at the
special inclusive pric.e of £2 2s. Od. strictly net.
The Registrar-General is empowered by Section 6 (2) of the Census Act
(Northern Ireland), 1925, to prepare and supply from the Census returns statistics
other than those published in the Census Reports., at the request and cost of any
local.authority or person. Any special demands for statistics more detailed than
those published (either as regards subject matter or area) can thus, as far as office
records permit, be satisfied.
Requests for special compilation of Census statistics
should be addressed to the Registrar-General, who will be pleased to advise and
furnish an estimate of cost
jUNEJ 1928 ..
GENERAL REGISTER OFFICE)
MURRAY STREET,
BELFAST.

## Page 196

PART I.
DEFINITIONS AND EXPLANATIONS OF
TERMS USED IN THIS VOLUME.
AREA.
The acreages given are as measured by the Ordnance Survey of 1899-1907.
They . ~xclude
'
In Tables l, 3-5 (a) foreshore and unnamed islands of less than one rood
in extent;
(b) land · on the shores of Laughs Erne, Ca.stlehulme,
and Macnean ;
(c) th~ larger rivers and lakes.
N~B.-While (b) and (c) above are not enclosed within townland boundaries
they form part of administrative areas. The extent of these excluded portions
is shown· for the county, county districts, and poor law unions in foot-notes to the
approprjate. tables.
POPULATION.
'l'lJ.e.pop:ulation comprises all persons, including Forces of the Crown, enumera ..

    ted as at midnight of Sunday the 18th-19th April, 1926, together with those who,
not having been enumerated elsewhere, arrived in port or were received into any
dwelling oh the morning of the 19th April before the Census returns were collected.
The population assigned to each area .is that actually present (as defined
above) within it, whether as permanent residents or temporary visitors, persons
in port but on ship board being classified to the adjacent area.
In general these definitions apply to the population as enumerated at previous
censuses, and shown in Table 2.
ADMINISTRATIVE
DIVISIONS.
The. County i?. divided into four county districts (one urban and three rural)
comprising 79 district electoral divisions, three of which are situate in the urban
district (see Table 3). The urban district electoral divisions are cotern1inous with
wards bearing the same name.
Three popr law unions are contained within the County-two wholly and one
partly; the remaining portion of the divided union being situate in Tyrone County.
The medical dispensary districts situate in the County number sixteen (see Table 4).
Poor law unions and medical dispensary districts are, respectively, superintendent
registrars' and registrars~ dis.~ricts. for the regi~tration of marriages, births, and
. deaths.
·
.
.
The County js also divided into twenty county electoral divisions (see Table
5).; and forms a single regional education area (see Table 12).

~
•
•

:;.

'
,.
