"'
•
: Apart fr9m the reconstitution of county electoral divisions and district
el~ctoral divisions in 1923, the changes which have taken place in the boundaries
of administrative divisions since 1911 are as follows:-
(a) Portions of Ely and Florenc~·.court Dispensary Districts transferred to
Holywell No. 1 District ·(1st ·October, 1912):
(b) Portions of Enniskillen Dispei1sary District transferred to Florence Court
and Lisbellaw Districts (1st October, 1912).


---

## Page 197

·-· -·
Vll.
(c) Lisbellaw Dispensary District abolished. and ifs ~rea partitioned between
Enniskillen, Fl~rence Court, and Tempo Districts (1st April, 1921).
(d) Enniskillen Poor Lavv ·union reduced by the exclusion therefrom of the
County Cavan portion (1st April, 1921).
(e) Irvinestown Poor Law Union enlarged by the inclusion of that portion
of the former Poor Law Union of Ballyshannon which is situate in
Northern Ireland (1st April, 19~1)_.
(f) Lisnaskea Poor Law Union enlarged by the inclusion of that portion of
the former Poor Law Union of Clones which is situate irt Northern
Ireland (1st. April, 1921) .
. (g) Belleek Rural District abolished and its area added to Irvinestown Rural
District (lst April, 1921).
(h) Clones No. 2 Rural District abolished and its area ·added to Lisnaskea
Rural District (Is{ April, 1921).
·
PARLIAMENTARY CONSTITUENCY.
The present parliamentary constituency was created by the Government .
o:f Ireland Act, 1920, and comprises the administrative Counties of Fermanagh
and Tyrone.
HOUSES.
1.
The term " House " denotes
(a) each structure (except institutions) which stands alone or ·1s separated
from another by a vertical party wall; and which is used or intended
partly or wholly for ha?itation;
·
·
(b) each institution (viz., hospital, sanatorium, nursing home, infirmary,
workhouse, asylum, religious or charitable community, residential
school or college, barracks· or other premises under Naval, Military,
Air Force, or Police discipline, prison or· other place of detention),
regardless of the number of buildings within its precincts ;
(c) each vessel, caravan, tent, or other movable dwelling ..
The 1911 figures for "Houses" do not include vessels, and each separate
building within the precincts of an institution was at that Census regarded as a
separate " House."
2.
The terms '' Boarciing ... House ~' and "Lodging-House" denote, in addition
to habitations so described, any private house containing three or more boarders
or ~odgers.
·
·
·
3. The term " Private liouse '' denot~s all 'habitations· other than
(a)
dwellings within the precincts of an institution ;
(b)
hotels, boarding-houses, and other such co1nmunal dwellings;
(c)
premises in which three or more boarders, lodgers, or trade assistants
reside;
·
'
(d)
residences occupied by virtue of the tenant's employment) (except_'
rectoFies and manses, and dwellings belonging to but detached
from domestic establishments) ;
~7
(e)
premises occupied' b:y caretakers;
.
(f)
ships, caravans, tent~, a~d other' movable. dwellings ;_
(g) barns, stables, and out-houses occupied on Census night.

'

'
'
'
: •• •
(

described at (c) and (d)' above.

## Page 198

viii.
4.
The term "Inhabited" denotes actual residence of a person or persons,
on Census night. "Uninhabited" houses, therefore, include those temporarily
vacant through the absence of the usual occupiers as well as those without tenants.
Enumerators were instructed to omit from their enumeration empty houses in
such an advanced state of decay that there was little likelihood that they would
again be made fit for habitation. In this matter they were required to use their
own judgment.
ROOMS.
1. The rooms enumerated are the usual living rooms, including bedrooms
and kitchens, but excluding sculleries (i.e., rooms used only for washing, cleaning,
or cooking), pantries, cloakrooms, landings, closets or bathrooms, or any ware-
house, office or shop rooms.
2. For the purpose of classifying families by roo1ns occupied (Table 8),
shared-room occupations-i.e., those in which two or 1nore families have
rooms in common,-have been rounded up to the next integral number of rooms,
Thus, three families occupying It, 2!, and 2! rooms in a 6-rootned house would
be shown as occupying 2, 3, and 3 rooms respectively. The extent of the rounding-
up process may be ascertained in a general way by reference to Table 7, in vvhich
the actual number of rooms is given.
Generally, in cases of 1 room occupations,
all persons living therein, whether related or not, have been enumerated as one
Census family.
FAMILIES.
I. The term " Family " denotes
(a) The inhabitants of each institution (see 1 (b) of" Houses" definition),
hotel, boarding-house, lodging-house, or ship.
(b) Each person or group of persons occupying separately the whole, or
part of, any dwelling other than as described at (a).
In 1911 the inhabitants of premises described at (a) above were in certain
cases enumerated as two or more families.
2.
"Private Families" mean all families enumerated in "Private Houses''
(see 3 of "Houses'' definition).
RATEABLE
VALUATION.
The figures shown are those for service of the year ended 31st March, 1927,
and have been supplied by the Commissioner of Valuation.
Rateable heredita~
ments include lands, buildings, tolls, railways, tram-roads, canals, fisheries,
turbaries, mines, quarries, waterworks, telegraphs, and telephones.
The figures
throughout exclude the valuation of telegraphs, fisheries, and (in certain cases)
railways and waterworks, the amounts concerned being aggregated and shown for
the county, county districts, and poor law unions in foot-notes to the appropriate
tables.
EDUCATION.·.
The classification under educational establishments (which was assisted
by reference to lists supplied for Census purposes by the Ministry of Education)
is as follows :-
1.
PUBLIC ELEMENTARY SCHOOLS.
Day and evening pupils are grouped
together in this class, the . question on the Census schedule not
requiring a separation as between day and evening in respect of
pupils attending public elementary schools.
2.
SECONDARY ScHOOLS, including preparatory departments and preparatory
schools.
This heading relates to day pupils only, and the schools
are limited to those recognised by the Ministry of Education.


---

## Page 199

ix.
3.
TECHNICAL ScHOOLS. The day pupils shown in this group comprise those
stated to be attending junior technical schools, day commercial
classes, and day classes for domestic economy and manual instruction
which were being held at certain centres in April, 1926. The evening
pupils comprise
those stated to be attending technical schools
or classes in the evening.
4.
OTHER EDUCATIONAL ESTABLISHMENTS (Day and Evening). These in-
clude secondary schools not recognised by the l\1inistry of Education,
universities, training colleges for teachers, agricultural schools,
commercial schools, home or private tuition, etc.
OCCUPATIONS.
The occupational classification is based on that used in the Reports on the
Census of England and Wales, 1921.*
It will be seen that there are thirty-two orders.
Each of these covers the
same occupa tiona! ground as the England and Wales orders.
Eleven of the
orders are divided into thirty-two sub-orders, each of which is comparable with
the corresponding England and Wales sub-order, with the following exceptions :-

INDUSTRIES.
The industrial classification is based on that used 1n the Reports on the
Census of England and Wales, 1921. *
It will be seen that there are twenty-two orders.
Each of these cover the
same industrial ground as the England and /Wales orders.
Nine of the orders
are divided into thirty-eight sub-orders, each of which is comparable with the

---

## Page 200

X.
BIRTHPLACES.
Persons stated to have been born in Ireland-the County name or "Irish
Free State'' not being inserted-are assun1ed to have been born in the Irish Free
State. Where nationality is given but birthplace is not, it has been assumed that
the person was born in the country of w·hich he or she is a national. All persons
of British nationality and unstated birthplace have been regarded as born in the
County of enumeration.
RELIGIOUS PROFESSIONS.
The religious professions, other than Ro1nan Catholic, Presbyterian, Church
of Ireland, and l\1ethodist, have been classified under the headings set out at foot
of Table 18, separate mention being made of all professions containing 10 or more
adherents.
The heading "Presbyterian" includes those who call the1nselves
by this name without qualification, together with those returned as n1embers of
the Established Church of Scotland.
lVIembers of the Church of England and
the Episcopal Church of Scotland have not been included under Church of Ireland
except where stated. The latter group includes all persons returned as " Church "
or " Episcopalian."
ORPHANHOOD.
In compiling statistics under this head, step-parents, foster-parents, and
adoptive ·parents, so stated, have been treated in the same manner as natural
parents. Where the information as to one parent is stated as "not known," that
parent has been assumed to be dead and the case dealt with accordingly. Where
information as to neither parent is given, the case has been placed in the "No
Statement'' group.
INFIRMITIES.
I. DEAF AND -DuMB, DuMB, BLIND.
The figures given are in respect of
persons so returned, except where the answers indicate only partial infirmity, as
"nearly blind."
2.
CRIPPLED.
In addition to those persons specifically described as
" Crippled," the figures given include all cases in which it is clearly indicated
that the person
(a) has suffered a total permanent loss of the use of arm, hand, leg, or
foot;
(b) is permanently crippled in. such a degree as to prevent the free
pursuit of most ordinary occupations.
The following cases, treated as "Crippled," indicate the variety of the terms
used in answering the question on the Census schedule :-
Arn"l-deformed, disabled;
hand-deformed; hip 'joitif disease; knee-
decayed, tubercular arthritis in ; leg-disabled, ·diseased bone in,
infirm, short ; on crutch ; paralysis-infantile, nervous, of arms and
side, of legs and back, of spine ; rheumatoid_ arthritis; spinal caries ;
unable to ·walk.
3.
MENTALLY AFFLICTED~
All cases returned, whatever .the degree of
affliction, _are included in the tables. The following terms were used in the
returns:-
Afflicted; feeble minded; imbecile; melancholia; mental debility; simple;
weak intellect.
4.
Persons suffering from more than one infirmity have been classified to
that which appears most likely to constitute the greater, or greatest, disability;
infirmities being graded for. this purpose from· greatest to least, thus :-·' ·
·Mentally afflicted; blind; crippled; deaf and dumb; dumb.


---

## Page 201

:d.
SPELLING
OF PLACE
NAMES.
The spelling of place names follows that in the 1911 Census volumes,
except that corrections have been made to secure agreement with the spelling on the
Ordn2;nce Survey maps where differences were revealed in the course of the -w-ork.
The spelling now may be regarded as generally following that adopted by the
Ordnance Survey D_epartroent.
CONVENTION
AS
TO
DOTS
(.)
AND
DASHES. (-).
Throughout this volume dots (.) are used in tabular matter to indicate nil (0),
and dashes (-) are used to indicate that information cannot or is not intended
to be given.
CORRECTION
OF CENSUS
DATA.
Every effort has been made to eliminate, as far as practicable, errors and
inaccuracies from the Census data. The process of correction cegan with the
Enumerators, who received full instructions as to the action to te taken in cases
of error or omission; it was continued by a system of check applied at each
stage of the office work ; and was completed by an intensive scrutiny and inter-
comparison of the tabulation sheets.
Jhis scrutiny and comparison revealed
a certain number of incongruities, which, when material, ·were investigated and
corrected in the light of the facts disclosed.
Errors of a minor nature
(i.e., such as could not affect the statistical value of the Census Tables) were
adjusted more or less arbitrarily.


---

## Page 202

xii.
PART II.
STATISTICAL NOTES.
The definitions, etc. contained in Part I of this volume apply with equal
force to the contents of these notes and to the tables contained in Part III. Roman
numerals are used in this volume for the Tables in Part II, and Arabic .numerals
for the tables in Part III.
AREA.
The area of Fermanagh County, exclusive of the larger rivers and lakes, and
of land on the shores of Laughs Erne, Castlehulme, and Macnean, is 653 square
miles or 12.5 per cent. of the area of Northern Ireland.
POPULATION.
The population numbers 57,984 persons, the smallest recorded. The decrease
over the past 15 years amounts to 3,852 persons, or 6.2 per cent., a continuation
of the downward movement recorded at each Census enumeration from 1851
onwards. The average annual rate of decrease has, howev~r, been lessening since

    1891, and that for the period 1911-1926 (0.4 per cent.) is the smallest since the
beginning of the downward trend.
(Figures for the earlier periods are shown
in Table 2).

(a.} Including the deaths of non-civilians of the County of Fermanagh which occurred outside Northern Ireland.
It has not been found possible to estimate the number of these deaths at all closely, but from the War mortality
lists of soldiers from Ireland, which were compiled by the War Office from regimental records, it appearsthat
for the whole of Northern Ireland the non-civilian deaths which occurred outside the country numbered about
10,000 during the last intercensal period, exclusive of the deaths of regular soldiers stationed outside the country
at the date of the 1911 Census.
While comparable figures of excess of births over deaths are not available
for periods prior to 1871, it is evident that the actual net outward movement during
the past 85 years has considerably exceeded the recorded decrease in population.
Complete information as to the size and direction of the emigration stream is lack-
ing, but for the period 1871-1911 it can be stated that about two-thirds of the net
outward movement is accounted for by emigration to places abroad, and that about
54 per cent. of the natives of County Fermanagh living elsewhere in Ireland were
domiciled in what is now the Irish Free State.
From Table II it will be seen that the one urban district in the County has
increased very slightly in population during the past 15 years, while rural districts
have decreased-the trend in the latter type of area following that exhibited in
the preceding intercensal period. The 1926 urban population constitutes &4 per
cent. of the whole, as against 7.8 per cent. in 1911, and 8.3 per cent. in 1901.

* For the entire constituency of Fermanagh and Tyrone there are 8 members of the Parliament of Northern Ireland

Parliamentary Constituency .-In considering the percentages shown in
Table III, regard should be given to the qualifications entitling a person to be
registered as an elector. In general, a man is qualified if 21 years of age or upwards,
with the req_uisite residence or business premises qualification: a woman, if 30
years of age or upwards, and if (1) entitled to be registered as a local government
elector in respect of the occupation of land or premises (not being a dwelling-
house) of a yearly value of not less than £5, or of a dwelling-house ; or (2) the wife
of a husband entitled to be' so registered.
It may be noted that a person may be registered as a parliamentary elector
In a constituency although not resident therein.
The question of the comparison of the number of parliamentary electors
. with population will be dealt with more fully in the General Report.

While the total number of houses in the County decreased by 6.5 per cent.,
the number of inhabited houses decreased by 5.5 per cent. only-this being due to
a considerable reduction in the nutnber of uninhabited houses, the 1926 :figure for
which is the smallest recorded since 1891. The fact that the rate of decrease in
inhabited houses is less than that in population evidences a change in housing
conditions to which further reference is made in the comment on Table V below.
The number of houses under construction is the highest recorded since 1861.
Persons per Inhabited House and Persons per Family. The average number
of persons per inhabited house, which showed a steady decrease from 1851 to 1911,
has again shown a decrease from 1911 to 1926. The average number of persons
per family has also shown a further decline.
In this connection it should be noted
that by " family " is meant Census family, which it will be seen from the definition
on page viii differs from the natural family of parents and children.
With regard to the habitation of houses by more than one family, there has
been little change during the past half century, the number of families per 100
inhabited houses at each Census enumeration from 1871 to 1926 being as follows :

    107, 105, 104, 103, 102, 102.

## Page 205

xv.
Housing in County Districts.-Table V shows that there has been a decrease
in the total number of houses in all county districts but Enniskillen Urban, where
a slight increase is shown. Comparison of this Table with Table II also shows
that since 1911 the proportion of inhabited houses to population has increased
in all county districts but Enniskillen Rural, where the process has been reversed.
The extent of sub-letting in the county districts is best indicated by the factor
"number of private families per inhabited private house," vvhich is readily obtain-
able from the figures of house accommodation contained in Table 7. It will be
seen that the number of families per 100 private houses is 102 for the County, and
that there is little variation from this average except in Enniskillen Urban District,
where the figure is 123. 96 per cent. of the families in the County occupy a whole
house each.
Of all the inhabited private houses in the County 99 per cent. are occupied
by single families and 44 per cent. contain 1 to 3 rooms.
Accommodation of Families and Persons.-The occupied room accommodation
in the County averages one room per person, the variation from this average
ranging between 1.11 in Enniskillen Urban District and 0.91 in Irvinestown Rural
District. A detailed analysis of the room accommodation, coupled with the size
of family, etc., appears in Table 8.
Over 29 per cent. of the families in the County
are seen to occupy 3 rooms each, the family of 3 occurs most frequently, and the
most common combination consists of families of 3, each family occupying 3 rooms.

Table VII, in conjunction with Table 8, will enable comparisons tobe made
between the density of occupation in ec:tch county district. In these tables the
density of 2 persons and less per room has not been sub-divided, but this is not
intended to imply any judgment as to vvhat in fact constitutes adequate accom-
modation, since this matter obviously could not be determined without a kno·wledge
of other factors, such as size of rooms, sanitary and water provision, etc. . Sub-
divisions other than those set out in summary form can readily be calculated from
the details given in the first nine columns of Table 8.
The average density of occupation in Enniskillen Urban District is less than
that in rural districts as a whole; but the distribution of urban and rural popula-
tion over the various densities differs considerably, the proportion of the private
family population occupying premises at a density of more than 2 persons per room
being 13.8 per cent. in EnniskillenUrban District as against 19.2 per cent. in the
rural districts. The greatest density of occupation is found ·in Irvinestown Rural
District, where 23.1 per cent. of the private family population are housed at a
density of more than 2 persons per room.

t Including divorced.
Excess of Males over Females.-Table 2 shows that the number of females

    per 1,000 males has fallen from 1,059 in 1821 to 926 in 1926. The preponderance
of males first recorded in 1891 has been growing steadily since. It will be seen
from Table II that the females now outnumber the males only between ages 70-94.

## Page 207

xvii.
Distribution according to Age.-The decrease of 6.2 per cent. in the total

    pop~lation is t~e net result of decreases in age groups0-9, 20-44, and 60 and over;
    and Increases 1n age groups 10-19, and 45-59. lVIaterials for an analysis of the
manner in which variations in the number of births and deaths from year to year,
the number of war deaths, and the movement of population have combined to
produce these changes in age distribution are not available : the effect has
been to reduce the average age of the population from approximately 32.5 years
in 1911 to 32 years at the present time.
Distribution by Marital Condition.-Table XII sho·ws the percentage decrease
in the male and female populations of each marital condition since 1911. It will
be seen that the widowed persons and single females have decreased at a greater
rate, and the married persons and single males at a lesser rate, than is shown for the
population as a whole (6.2 per cent.)

From Table 12 it will be seen that of the total population in the County 18.1
per cent. of the males and 19.0 per cent. of the females are attending educational
establishments.
Of the children aged 6 to 13 inclusive, 5.3 per cent. of boys and 6.0 per cent.
of girls are not stated to be attending schooL
At ages 15 to 19 inclusive, the persons still attending various kinds of educa-
tional establishments represent 10.6 per cent. of the males and 16.2 per cent. of
the females.
In endeavouring to draw conclusions from this table it will be necessary to
treat the Census :figures relating to persons attending technical schools with con-
siderable reserve, pending further investigation, since a marked difference has been
found to exist between them and figures of pupils enrolled in technical schools as
published by the Ministry of Education.
Further reference to this matter will be
made in the General Report on the Census relating to Northern Ireland as a whole.
OCCUPATIONS.
Proportion Occupied.-From Table XIII it will be seen that both for males
and females the percentage of the total population occupied is slightly higher
than in 1911.

Occupational Status and National Health Insuranee.-A consideration of the
percep.tages in the following table, which has been compiled from Table 14, shows
that between a third and a half of the occupied males are either employers or
workers on own account, the proportion for occupied females being 1 in 3.
Of
the employees, apprentices, and out of work taken together, those who were
returned as out of work at the date of the Census represent 5 per cent. in the case
of males and 6 per cent. in the case of females.

Of all the occupied population 18.1 per cent. of the males and 27.1 pe:r cent.
of the females are returned as being entitled to benefits under the National Health
Insurance Acts. For employers and workers on own account of all ages, taken
together, the percentages are 1.0 per: cent. of the males and 0.6 per cent. of the
females, and for employees, apprentices, and out of work, taken together, they
are 30.0 per cent. of the males and 40.0 per cent. of the females ..
Of the unoccupied, including retired, 2.1 per cent. of the males and 0.4 per
cent. of the females are returned as entitled to benefits.

BIRTHPLACES.
Table 17 shows, by sex, the numbers of persons born in each of the counties
of Northern Ireland, other countries in the British Isles_, overseas Dominions,
foreign countries, and at sea.
Of the total population of County Fermanagh
82 per cent. ·were born in the County, 3 per cent. in the adjace11t County of Tyrone,
and 2 per cent. in the remaining parts of Northern Ireland. Only 0.43 per cent.
were born outside the British Isles, and over one third of these ·were torn in the
British Dominions and Colonies.
Persons born in foreign countries numbered
only 162.

numbered 5 persons born in the Isle of Man and 1 in the Channel Islands.
From Table XVIII it will be seen that the proportion of the population born
in Northern Ireland has gradually decreased since 1861, while the proportion born
in the Irish .Free State has steadily increased and now forms over 10 per cent. of
the whole. The proportions for other parts of the British Isles have also increased.
The proportion born abroad is a little less than in 1911.
RELIGIOUS PROFESSIONS.
It will be seen from Table XIX that Presbyterians and Other and Not Stated
Denominati?ns have ~ncreased in ~umbers, and no'Y form greater proportions of
the population than In 1911 ; while Roman Cathohcs, Protestant Episcopalians
and Methodists are both absolutely and relatively less numerous. The greatest
absolute decrease occurred among Roman Catholics, and the greatest relative
decrease among Methodists.

ORPH~NHOOD.
Table 19 shows that of the .17,724 children enumerated in the County at
ages under 16, 1,940 have lost one or both parents. This iigure excludes cases of
orphaned children whose loss has been supplied by step-parents or adoptive parents,
and 43 cases in which no statement as to parents was secured.
Of the 1,940
orphans, 56 per cent. are fatherless and 37 per cent. are 1notherless, the remaining
7 per cent. beirig orphaned by the death of both parents. The high proportion
of fatherless to motherless orphans is in part a corollary of the high proportion of
widows to widowers, though to some extent ·it n1ay be due to a possible tendency
to regard the fathers of illegitimate children as dead if their whereabouts are
unknown.
·
INFIRMITIES.
The number of persons returned as both Deaf and Dun1b is 20, or 10 less than
the number returned in 1911. The Dumb Only numbered 11, which compares
with 24 in 1911.

    The number returned as Blind has increased from 48 in 1911 to 69 in 1926.
The total nu1nber of persons who have been classified as Crippled is 272,
comprising 152 males and 120 females.
It has not been found possible to test the
reliability of this figure, and it should be accepted with due reserve, both on
account of the broad interpretation which necessarily had to be placed on the
term "crippled" for classification purposes, and because of a possible reluctance
on the part of some crippled persons to disclose their infirmity by stating it on
the census schedule.
Of the 110 persons returned as mentally afflicted only 1 was enumerated in an
institution. · In 1911 the persons returned as Lunatic or Idiot numbered 99.
(It will be seen fro1n Table 9 that there is no Lunatic Asylum in the County).
Particulars of the sex, age, and marital condition of persons suffering from
infinnities will be found in Table 20.

(a) Two Dispensary Districts in County Cavan, formerly .part of Enniskillen Union, are no longer included therein.
(b) Ely District was altered by transfer of a portion to Holywell No. 1 District (1st October, 1912).
.
.
(c) Enniskillen District was altered by transfers of territory to Florence Court and Lisbellaw Districts (1st October, 1912), and from Lisbellaw on the abolition. of that D1Str1ct
(1st April, 1921).
.
(d) Florence Court District was altered by transfers of territory to Rolywell No. 1 District (1st October, 1912), and from Enniskillen a.nd Liabellaw Districts (let Octoberi .1912 and
1st April, 1921 respectively).
(e) Holywell No. 1 District was altered by transfer of portions of Ely and Florence Court Districts (1st October, 1912).

CENSUS OF NORTHERN IRELAND, 1926.
TABLE 5.-Area, Population, Houses,
Accommodation, and Valuation.
County, County Electoral Divisions, and
District Electoral Divisions.
DEFINITIONS.-
1. Definitions of the terms " Area," "Population/' " Houses," "Inhabited," "Private Families," "Rooms," and " Rateable
Valuation" will be found on pages vi to viii.
2. The 1911 figures throughout are fOl' the County and its adrninistrative divisions as constituted at the date of the 1926 Census.
(See note on Administrative Divisions, page vi.)
3. Dots (.) indicate nil (0), and dashes (-) indicate that information cannot or is not intended to be given.
ARRANGEMENT.-!. County Electoral Divisions are in alphabetical order, and, under each of these, District Electoral Divisions are arranged in
a similar order.
2. The reference in the second column of the Table is to the serial number prefixed to the name of each District Electoral
Division in the first column of Table 3.
ABBREVIATIONS.-C.E.D.=County Electoral Division : D.E.D.=District Electoral Division.

## Page 279

iii.
PREFACE.
This volume has been prepared in accordance with the provisions of
Section 6 (1) of the Census Act (Northern Ireland), 1925.
The 1926 Census
statistics which it contains were compiled from the returns made as at midnight

    of the 18-19th April, 1926: they supersede those in the Preliminary Report
published in August, 1926, and may be regarded as final.
The Census publications will consist of:-
1.
SEVEN CouNTY VoLUMES, each similar in design and scope to the present
publ~ca tion.
2.
A GENERAL REPORT relating to :.Northern Ireland as a whole, covering
in more detail the statistics shown in the County Volumes, and
containing in addition tables showing
(i.) the occupational distribution of persons engaged in each of
51 groups of industries ;
(ii.) the distribution of the foreign born population by nationality,
age, marital condition, and occupation ;
(iii.) the distribution of families of dependent children ·under 16
years of age, by age, sex, marital condition, and occupation of
parent;
(iv.) the occupational distribution of persons suffering from infirmities.
3.
A ToPOGRAPHICAL INDEX, dealing in separate alphabetical sections with
each type of area for which statistics are published in the County
series.
The particulars given for each territorial unit include references
to the 6" Ordnance Survey maps and to Tables 3, 4, or 5 of the
relative County Volume.
· The net prices for the above publications will be 5s. Od. for each County
Volume and lOs. Od. each for the General Report and the Topographical Index.
A complete set
of
these Volumes may be obtained directly from the
Superintendent, H.M. Stationery Office, 15, Donegall Square West, Belfast, at the
special inclusive price of £2 2s. Od. strictly· net.
The Registrar-General is empowered by Section 6 (2) of the Census Act
(Northern Ireland), 1925, to prepare and supply from the Census returns, statistics
other than those published in the Census Reports at the request and cost of any
local authority or person.
Any.special demands for statistics more detailed than
those published (eithe~ as regards subject matte_r or area) c~nthus, as far as ?~ce
records permit, be satisfied.
Requests for special compilation of Census statistics
should be addressed to the Registrar-General, who will be pleased· to advise and
furnish an estimate of cost.
jUNE, 1928.
GENERAL REGISTER OFFICE,
MURRAY STREET,
BELFAST.

---

## Page 282

vi.
PART I.
DEFINITIONS AND EXPLANATIONS OF
TERMS USED IN THIS VOLUME.
AREA.
The acreages given are as measured by the Ordnance Survey of 1899-1907.
They exclude-
In Tables 1, 3-5 (a) Foreshore and unnamed islands of less than one
rood in extent.
In Tables 3-5
(b) The larger rivers, lakes, and tideways.
N.B.-While the larger rivers, lakes, and tideways are not enclosed within
townland boundaries they form part of administrative areas. The extent of these
excluded portions is shown for the county, county borough, county districts, and
poor law unions in footnotes to the appropriate tables.
POPULATION.
The population comprises all persons, including Forces of the Crown,

    enumerated as at midnight of Sunday the 18th-19th April, 1926, together with
those who, not having been enumerated elsewhere, arrived in port or were
received into any dwelling on the morning of the 19th April before the Census
returns were collected.
The population assigned to each area is that actually present (as defined
above) within it, whether as permanent residents or temporary visitors, persons
in port but on ship board being classified to the adjacent area.
In general these definitions apply to the population as enumerated at previous
censuses, and shown in Table 2.
ADMINISTRATIVE
DIVISIONS.
The County is divided into seven county districts (three urban and four rural)
comprising 86 district electoral divisions, three of which are situate in urban
districts (see Table 3).
The County Borough is divided into five wards, each of which is coterminous
with a district electoral division (see Table 3).
Four poor lavv unions are contained within the County and County Borough.
The medical dispensary districts situate in the County and County Borough
number twenty-one and three respectively (see Table 4).
Poor law unions and
medical dispensary districts are, respectively, superintendent registrars' and
registrars' districts for the registration of marriages, births, and deaths.
The County is also divided into nineteen county electoral divisions (see Table
5); and into three regional education areas.
The County Borough forms an
additional regional education area (see Table 12).
·
Apart from the reconstitution of county electoral divisions and district elec-
toral divisions in 1923, the changes which have taken place in the boundaries of
administrative divisions since 1911 are as follovvs :-.
(a) Portion of Londonderry County transferred to Antri1n County consequent
on the extension of Portrush Urban District (1st April, 1915).
(b) Coleraine Rural District, Coleraine Union, and Portstewart Medical
Dispensary District reduced by transfer to Antrim County of the
portion referred to at (a) (1st April, 1915).


---

## Page 283

vii.
(c) Portstewart Urban District formed out of portion of Coleraine Rural
District (1st April, 1916).
(d) Londonderry Poor Law Union reduced by the exclusion therefrom of the
County Donegal portion (1st April, 1921).
PARLIAMENTARY CONSTITUENCY.
The present parliamentary constituency was created by the Governn1ent
of Ireland Act, 1920, and comprises the administrative county and county borough
of Londonderry.
HOUSES.
1.
The term " House " denotes
(a) each structure (except institutions) which stands alone or is separated
from another by a vertical party wall ; and ·which is used or intended
partly or wholly for habitation ;
(b) each institution (viz., hospital, sanatorium, nursing horne, infirmary,
workhouse, asylum, religious or charitable comn1unity, residential
school or college, barracks or other premises under Naval, l\1ilitary,
Air Force, or Police discipline, prison or other place of detention),
regardless of the number of buildings within its precincts ;
. (c) each vessel, caravan, tent, or other n1.ovable dwelling.
The 1911 figures for "Houses" do not include vessels, and each separate
building within the precincts of an institution was at that Census regarded as a
separate "House."
2.
The terms " Boarding-House " and " Lodging-House " denote, in addition
to habitations so described, any private house containing three or more boarders
or lodgers.
3.
(a)
(b)
(c)
(d)
(e)
(f)
(g)
The term " Private House " denotes all habitations other than
dwellings within the precincts of an institution ;
hotels, boarding-houses, and other such communal dwellings ;
premises in which three or more boarders~ lodgers, or trade assistants
reside;
residences occupied by virtue of the tenant's employment, (except
rectories and manses, and dwellings belonging to but detached
from domestic establishments) ;
premises occupied by caretakers;
ships, caravans, tents, . and other movable dwellings ;
barns, stables, and out-houses occupied on Census night.
The term includes dwellings over shops except when these· are occupied as
described at (c) and (d) above.
4.
The term "Inhabited" denotes actual residence of a person or persons,
on Census night.
" Uninhabited " houses therefore, include those temporarily
vacant through the absence of the usual occupiers as well as those without tenants.
Enumerators were instructed to omit frorn their enumeration empty houses in
such an advanced state of decay that there was little likelihood that they would
again be rnade fit forhabitation. In this matter they were required to use their
own judgment.
ROOMS.
1. The rooms enumerated are the usual living rooms, including bedrooms
and kitchens, but excluding sculleries (i.e., rooms used only for washing, cleaning,
· or cooking), pantries, cloakrooms, landings, closets or bathrooms, or any ware-
house, office or shop roorns.


---

## Page 284

viii.
2.
For the purpose of classifying families by rooms occupied (Table 8),
shared-room occupations-i.e., those in which two or more families have
rooms in co1nmon,-have been rounded up to the next integral number of rooms.
Thus, three families occupying It, 2!, and 2! rooms in a 6-roomed house would
be shown as occupying 2, 3, and 3 rooms respectively.
The extent of the rounding-
up process may be ascertained in a general way by reference to Table 7, in which
the actual number of rooms is given.
Generally in cases of I room occupations,
all persons living therein, whether related or not, have been enu1nerated as one
Census family.
FAMILIES.
The term " Family " denotes
(a)
The inhabitants of each institution (see I (b) of "Houses" definition),
hotel, boarding-house, lodging-house, or ship;
(b)
Each person or group of persons occupying separately the whole, or part
of, any dwelling other than as described at (a).
" Private Families " mean all families enumerated in " Private Houses "
(see 3 of "Houses" definition) ; and from the population and family totals of
this class is calculated the number of persons per family in I926 (see Table VI).
In 1911 the inhabitants of premises described at (a) above ·were in certain
cases enumerated as tvvo or more families.
The number of persons per family
was obtained by dividing the number of Census families into the entire population
less the cc special inmates" (i.e., patients, prisoners, soldiers, etc.) of institutions.
RATEABLE
VALUATION.
The figures shown are those for service of the year ended 31st March, 1927,
and have been supplied by the Commissioner of Valuation.
Rateable heredita-
ments include lands, buildings, tolls, railways, tram-roads, canals, fisheries,
turbari~s, mines, quarries, waterworks, telegraphs, and telephones. The figures
throughout exclude the valuation of electric and water mains, telegraphs, tele-
phones, fisheries, and (in certain cases) railways, and waterworks, the amounts
concerned being aggregated and shown for the county and county borough, county
districts, and poor law unions in foot-notes to the appropriate tables.
EDUCATION.
The classification under educational establishments (which was assisted
by reference to lists supplied for Census purposes by the Ministry of Education)
is as follows :-
1.
PuBLIC ELEMENTARY ScHoOLS.
Day and evening pupils are grouped
together in this class, the question on the Census schedule not
requiring a separation as between day and evening in respect of
pupils attending public elementary schools.
2.
SECONDARY ScHOOLS, including preparatory departments and preparatory
schools.
This heading relates to day pupils only, and the schools
are limited to those recognised by the Ministry of Education.
3.
TECHNICAL ScHOOLS.
The day pupils shown in this group comprise those
stated to be attending junior technical schools, day commercial
classes, and day classes for domestic economy and manual instruction
which v;rere being held at certain centres in April, .1926.
The evening
pupils comprise those stated to be attending technical schools or
classes in the evening.
4.
OTHER EDUCATIONAL ESTABLISHMENTS (Day and Evening).
These in-
clll:de s~c.ondary ~c~ools not recognised by the Ministry of Education,
un1vers1t~es, tra1n1ng colleges for teachers, agricultural schools,
com1nercial schools, home or private tuition, etc.

ix.

The occupational classification is based on that used in the Reports on the
Census of England and Wales, 1921. *
It will be seen that there are thirty-two orders.
Each of these covers the
same occupational ground as the England and \iVales orders.
Eleven of the
orders are divided into thirty-two sub-orders, each of which is comparable with
the corresponding England and Wales sub-order, ·with the following exceptions:-

Within the sub-orders are 341 occupational headings, evolved by processes
both of grouping and expansion from the England and Wales headings, which
number 611.
INDUSTRIES.
The industrial classification is based on that used 1n the Reports on the
Census of England and Wales, 1921.*
It will be seen that there are twenty-two orders.
Each of these covers the
same industrial ground as the England and Wales orders.
Nine of the orders
are divided into thirty-eight sub-orders, each of which is comparable with the
corresponding England and Wales sub-order, with the following exceptions:-

Within the sub-orders are 226 industrial headings, evolved by processes both
of grouping and expansion from the England and Wales headings, which number
477. The Industry table contained in this volume shows figures for orders and
sub-orders only.
BIRTHPLACES.
Persons stated to have been .born in Ireland-the County name or ''Irish
Free State " not being inserted-are assumed to have been born in the Irish Free
State. Where nationality is given but birthplace is not, it has been assumed
that the person was born in the country of which he or she is a national. All persons
of British nationality and unstated birthplace have been regarded as born in the
County of enumeration.
RELIGIOUS
PROFESSIONS.
The religious professions, other than Ron1an Catholic, Presbyterian, Church
of Ireland, and Methodist, have been classified under the headings set out at foot
of Table 18, separate mention being made of all professions containing 10 or more
adherents. The heading " Presbyterian " includes those \vho call themselves
by this name without qualification, together with those returned as members of
• Full details ·of the English system of classification are published in the "ClassHication of Occupations,
Census, 1921," and the "Classification of Industries, Census, 1921," to be purchased directly from H.M.S.O.,
or through any Bookseller.
·

## Page 286

X.
the Established Church of Scotland.
Other Presbyterian denominations are shown
at the foot of the table.
Members of the Church of England and the Episcopal
Church of Scotland have not been included under Church of Ireland except where
stated. The latter group includes all persons ·returned as "Church" or
" Episcopalian."
ORPHANHOOD.
In compiling statistics under this head, step-parents, foster-parents, and
adoptive parents, so stated, have been treated in the same manner as natural
parents.
Where the infonnation as to one parent is stated as "not known," that
parent has been assumed to be dead and the case dealt with accordingly.
Where
information as to neither parent is given, the case has been placed in the " No
Statement " group.
INFIRMITIES.
1.
DEAF AND DuMB, DuMB, BLIND.
The figures given are in respect of
persons so returned, except where the answers indicate only partial infirmity, as
" nearly blind."
2.
CRIPPLED.
In addition to those persons specifically described as
" Crippled" the figures given include all cases in which it is clearly indicated
that the person
(a) has suffered a total permanent loss of the use of arm, hand, leg, or
foot;
(b) is permanently crippled in such a degree as to prevent the free
pursuit of most ordinary occupations.
The following cases, treated as "Crippled," indicate the variety of the terms
used in answering the question on the Census schedule :-
·
Arm-deformed, disabled; hand-deformed; hip joint disease;
knee-
decayed, tubercular arthritis in ; leg-disabled, diseased bone in,
'infirm, short; on crutch; paralysis-infantile, nervous, of arms and
side, of legs and back, of spine; rheumatoid arthritis; spinal caries;
unable to walk.
3.
MENTALLY AFFLICTED.
All cases returned, whatever the degree of
affliction, are included in the tables.
The following terms were used in the
returns:-
Afflicted; feeble minded; imbecile; melancholia; mental debility; simple;
weak intellect.
4.
Persons suffering from more than one infirmity have been classified to
that which appears most likely to constitute the greater, or greatest, disability,
infirmities being graded for this purpose from greatest to least, thus:-
Mentally afflicted ; blind ; crippled ; deaf and dumb ; dumb.
SPELLING
OF PLACE
NAMES.
The spelling of pla~e names follows that in the 1911 Census volumes,
except that corrections have been made to secure agreement with the spelling on the
Ordnance Survey maps where differences were revealed in the course of the work.
The spelling now may be regarded as generally following that adopted by the
Ordnance Survey Department.
CONVENTION
AS
TO
DOTS
(.)
AND
DASHES (-).
Throughout this volume dots (.) are used in tabular matter to indicate nil (Q),
and dashes (-) are used to indicate that information cannot or is not intended
to be given.


---

## Page 287

xi.
CORRECTION
OF
CENSUS
DATA.
Every effort has been made to eliminate, as far as practicable, errors and
inaccuracies from the Census data.
The process of correction began with the
Enumerators, who received full instructions as to. the action to be taken in cases
of error or omission; it was continued by a system of check applied at each
stage of the office work ; and was completed by an intensive scrutiny and inter-
comparison of the tabulation sheets.
This scrutiny and comparison revealed
a certain number of incongruities, which, when material, were. investigated and
corrected in the light of the facts disclosed.
Errors of a minor nature (i.e., such
as could not affect the statistical value of the Census Tables) were adjusted more ot
less arbitrarily.


---

## Page 288

xii.
PART. II.
STATISTICAL NOTES .
.
The definitions, etc., contained jn Part I of this volume apply with equal
force to the contents of these notes and to the tables contained in Part III. Roman
numerals are used in this volume for the tables in Part II, and Arabic numerals
for the tables in Part III.
AREA.
The area of Londonderry County and County Borough, exclusive of the
larger rivers, lakes, and tideways, is 804 square miles, or 15.3 per cent. of the area
of Northern Ireland.
In 1915 the County lost about one-third of a square mile to
County Antrim (see note to Table 2).
POPULATION.
The population numbers 139,693 persons, having decreased by 932 persons
since the date of the 1911 Census, when the population numbered 140,625 persons.
This decrease is a continuation (though at a much smaller rate) of the downward
movement recorded at each Census enumeration from 1851 onwards.
The actual
decrease, after adjustment on account of the transfer of portion of the County to
County Antrim, amounts only to 663 persons.

(a) The populations given relate to the County and County Borough as constituted at the beginning of the
intercensal period, and the births and deaths to the County and County Borough as constituted at the time of
their registration.
A portion of the County of Londonderry was transferred to the County of Antrim in
1915, consequent on the extension of Pcrtrush Urban District.
(b) Including the deaths of non-civilians of the County and County Borough of Londonderry which occurred
outside Northern Ireland. It has not been found possible to estimate the number of these deaths at all
closely, but"from the War mortality lists of soldiers from Ireland, which were compiled by the War Office
from regimental records, it appears that for the whole of Northern Ireland the non-civilian deaths which
occurred outside the country numbered about 10,000 during the last intercensal period, exclusive of the deaths
of regular soldiers stationed outside the country at the date of the 1911 Census.
While comparable figures of excess of births over deaths are not available
for periods prior to 1871, it is evident that the actual net outward movement
from the County and County Borough during the past 85 years was much
larger than the recorded decrease in population.
Complete information as to
the size and direction of the emigration stream is lacking, but for the period
1891-1911 it can be stated that emigration to places abroad accounted for
approximately two-thirds of the net outward movement, and that 79 per cent.
of the natives of Londonderry living elsewhere ·in Ireland were. domiciled in
the area now comprised by Northern Ireland.
From Table II it will be seen that urban districts as a whole have increased in
population during the past 15 years, whilst rural districts as a whole have
decreased-the trend in each aggregate following that exhibited in the preceding
intercensal period.


---

## Page 289

xiii.
The population of the County Borough has increased by 10.7 per cent.
during the past 15 years, the increase varying in the wards between 7.1 per cent.
and 16.4 per cent.
The loss in population sustained by East Ward during 1901-

    1911 has been virtually regained during 1911-1926, and in three of the four
remaining wards the average annual rate of increase during the last intercensal
period has been much greater than during 1901-1911.
Taking county districts and County Borough togeth~j·, the 1926 urban popu-

    lation constitutes 41.3 per cent. of the whole as against 36.4 per cent. in 1911, and
34.3 per cent. in 1901-the percentages quoted relating to urban areas as
constituted at the date of each Census.

The persons per acre rates vary in urban districts ·between 8.0 for Coleraine and
1.3 for Portstewart; and in the County Borough. between 106.2 for West 'Ward
and 12.9 for Waterside Ward. Such rates, however, should be used with reserve
in measuring the degree of population density in urban areas, since the acreage
of these may include widely differing proportions of waste land, land under
pasture, etc. In rural districts the rates vary from 0.1 to 0.2 persons per acre.
Tables 3, 4, and 5 provide more detailed information regarding the population
in the various sub-divisions of the County and County Borough, and incorporate
figures of rateable valuation. The rateable valuation has increased during the past

    fifteen years from £437,334 to £454, 780-an increase of 4.0 per cent. as compared
with a decrease of 0.5 per cent. in population.
(These figures refer to the County
and County Borough as constituted at the date of the 1926 Census).
Parliamentary Constituency.-In considering the percentages shown in
Table III, regard should be given to the qualifications entitling a person to be
registered as an elector. In general, a man is qualified if 21 years of age or upwards,
with the requisite residence or business premises qualification: a woman, if 30
years of age or upwards, and if (1) entitled to be registered as a local government
elector in respect of the occupation of land or pre1nises (not being a dwelling-
house) of a yearly value of not less than £5, or of a dwelling-house ; or (2) the wife
of a husband entitled to be so registered.

* The :figures given relate to the County and County Borough as constituted at the date of each Census.
A portion of the County of Londonderry was transferred to the County of .\ntrim in 1915, consequent on
the extension of Portrush Urban District.
While the total number of houses decreased by 5.2 per cent., the number of
inhabited houses decreased by 2.1 per cent. only-this being due to a considerable
reduction in the number of uninhabited houses, the 1926 figure for which is the
smallest recorded since 1871.
The fact that the rate of decrease in inhabited
houses is greater than that in population evidences a change in housing conditions
to which further reference is made in the comment on Table V below.
The number of houses under construction is the highest recorded since 1831,
and more than twice that in 1911.
Persons per Inhabited House and Persons per Family.-The average number
both of persons per inhabited house and persons per family declined more or less
steadily from 1851 to 1911.
An increase in the former figure was registered in
1926,. while in the latter a further slight fall was recorded.
In this connection it
should be noted that by "family" is meant Census family, which it will be seen


---

## Page 291

XV.
from the definition on page viii, differs from the natural family of parents and
children.
With regard to the habitation of houses by more than one family, there has
been little change during the past half-century, the number of families per 100
inhabited houses at each Census enumeration from 1871 to 1926 being as follows :

    106, 105, 105, 103, 103, 107. The slight increase in the figure during the last
intercensal period follows from the fact that the proportion of families to popu-
lation has increased while that of inhabited houses to population has decreased.

(a) The :figures given relate to each county district and ward as constituted at the date of the 1926 Census.
Housing in County Districts and Wards.-Table V shows that there has been
an increase in the total number of houses in two county districts and four wards,
and a decrease in five county districts and one ward.
Comparison of this Table
with Table II also shows that since 1911 the proportion of inhab~ted houses to
population has increased in four of the seven county districts, the process being
reversed in the other three (Limavady Urban and Rural, and Londonderry Rural).
The greatly lessened proportion of inhabited houses to population in each ward
of the County Borough indicates that housing conditions there are definitely worse
than in 1911.
. The extent of sub-letting in the various county districts and wards is best
indicated by the factor" number of private families per 100 private houses," which
is readily obtainable from the figures of house accommodation contained in Table 7.
For the county districts the nun1ber of private families per 100 private houses
averages 101, there being little variation from this average. For the County

    Borough the figure is 125, the rate ranging between 139 in West Ward and 115 in·
North Ward. Only 65 per cent. of the families in the County Borough occupy
a whole house each, as against 98 per cent. in the County.
In the county districts as a whole, 99 per cent. of the inhabited private houses
are occupied by single families and 50 per cent. contain one to three rooms: in the
County Borough as a whole, 81 per cent. of the inhabited private houses. are occupied
by single families, and 61 per cent. contain four to five rooms.

    (a) Families.-For 1926, Census Families residing in private houses only were ta.ken, but for 1901 and 1911
all Census families were included, it being impracticable to segregate the figures for private families.

    (b) Persons.-For 1926, the population in private houses only was taken, and for 1901 and 1911 that taken was
the total population less the inmates of institutions.
Accommodation of Families and Persons.-The slight difference in treatment
necessarily employed in dealing with the 1901 and 1911 families and persons
figures in Table VI has but a small effect upon comparisons between the three

    Census years, the 4.69 persons per family in 1926 becoming 4.96 if adjusted to
agree with the :figures for the previous years.
A comparison of the 1926 with
the 1911 figures of accommodation of private families reveals a decided
movement towards smaller sized units of occupation, 68 per cent. now consisting
of l to 4 rooms as against 55 per cent. in 1911.
The percentage of persons in
occupation of 1 to 4 roomed units shows an even greater increase, i.e., from 49

    per cent. in 1911 to 65 per cent. in 1926.
With regard to the size of .families in relation to units of occupation, we find
that whilst on the average the 1926 census family is smaller than that in 1901
and 1911, the sizes of families occupying 4 rooms and less are in general larger than
the corresponding families in those years. It will also be seen that the average size
of families occupying 5 or more rooms is appreciably less than in 1901 and 1911.
The occupied room accommodation in the county districts averages 0.91 rooms
per person, the variation from this average ranging between 1.77 in Portstewart
Urban and 0.82 in Limavady Rural.
In urban districts room accommodation
is above the mean; in rural districts generally below it.
In the County Borough
the occupied room accommodation averages 0.86 rooms per person, and varies
from 1.07 in North Ward to 0.65 in West Ward.
A detailed analysis of the room accommodation coupled with the size of family,
etc., appears in _'~a~le 8.
In the County (excluding the ~ounty Borough) 26 per·
cent. of the ~am1hes occupy 2 rooms each, the family of 2 occurs most frequently,
and the most common combination consists of families of 2, each family occupying
2 rooms. In the County Borough 30 per cent. of the families occupy 4 rooms each,
the family of 3 occurs most frequently, and the most common combination
consists of families of 4, each family occupying 4 rooms.

Table VII, in conjunction with Table 8, will enable comparisons to be made
between the density of occupation in each county district and ward. In these
tables the density of 2 persons and less per room has not been sub-divided, but this
is not intended to imply any judgment as to what in fact constitutes adequate
accommodation, since this matter obviously could not be determined without a
knowledge of other factors, such as size of rooms, sanitary and water provision,
etc.
Sub-divisions other than those set out in summary form can readily. be
calculated from the details given in the first nine columns of Table 8.
In the county districts as a whole 26.5 per cent. of the private family popula-
tion occupy premises at a density of more than 2 persons per room. The average
density of occupation in urban districts is appreciably less than in rural districts,
and the distribution of the urban and rural population over the various densities
exhibit marked differences, the proportion of the private family population occupy-
ing premises at a density of more than 2 persons per room being 16.1 per cent.
in the urban districts as against 28.0 per cent. in the rural districts. The greatest
density of occupation is found in Limavady Rural District, where 32.2 per cent.
of the private family population are housed. at a density of more than 2 persons
per room.
In the County Borough as a whole 25.2 per cent. of the private family popu-
. lation occupy premises at a density of more than 2 persons per room, the greatest
density of occupation being found in ·west Ward, where 40.3 per cent. of the private
family population occupy premises at a density of more than 2 persons per room.
Comparison of Units of Occupation with Inhabited Private Houses.-Another
comparison which may usefully be made is that shown in Table VIII, the figures
for which have been extracted from Tables 7 and 8.

## Page 294

XVlll.
In the foregoing table, which excludes uninhabited houses, a classification
of families according to the number of rooms they occupy is shown side by side
with a separate classification of Inhabited Private I-Iouses according to the number
of rooms which they contain. It will be seen that whilst there are 3,292 fan1ilies
in the 1.:.3 room group there are only 683 houses in that group.
In the remaining
groups the numbers of houses are greater than the nun1bers of families.
Apart from all questions as to size of rooms, etc. a hypothetical re-arrange-
ment of families to bring their room accommodation into direct relation with that
of the houses available would therefore still leave 2,609 fan1ilies of the 1-3 room
group in occupation of a part of a house only in each case.
·
It will be seen from the figures for the County Borough on page 35 that under
the .conditions which in fact exist 3,042 of the total number of families are in
occupation of only a part of a house.
If the aim be to secure a greater degree of family privacy, the existence of
many cases of shared houses suggests the need for the provision either of additional
houses each containing a relatively small number of rooms, or, the provision of
separate premises in larger structurally sub-divided buildings.
In order to
determine the type of house to be erected in future, however, many factors need
to be taken into consideration.
In this connection special· attention is drawn to
the figures relating to the County Borough on page 40, from which it will be seen
that of the families in occupation of 1-3 rooms, 414 out of the total of 3,292
comprise only one person each, on the other hand, however, 395 of these families
each comprised 7 or more persons.
INSTITUTIONS, ETC.
The population in each of the types of inhabited buildings, etc., other than
private houses, is shown in Tables 9 and 10, together with the number of inmates
in respect of institutions.
The institutions are not individually identified as in
1911, but the number of institutions in each category is given.
The inmates of

    the Poor Law Tnstitution number 630 as against 651 at the date of the 1911
Census.

    Persons on shipboard number 159 as against 132 in 1911. "Other
premises" include police and military barracks, residential schools, premises
occupied by caretakers, etc.

Distribution according to Age.-The decrease of 0.7 per cent. in the total

    population is the net result of decreases in age groups 5-14, 20-24, 30-44, 65-84,
    and 90 and qver; and increases in age groups 0-4, 15-19, 25-29, 45-64, and 85-89.
Materials for an analysis of the manner in which variations in the number of births
and deaths fro1n year to year, the number of war deaths, and the movement of
population have combined to produce these changes in age distribution are not
available : the effect has been to lower the average age of the population from

    approximately 30.7 years in 1911 to 30.1 years at the present time.
Distribution by Marital Condition.-Table XII shows the percentage variations
in the male and female population of each marital condition since 1911.
The single
females and widowed persons have decreased at a greater rate, and the single
males at a lesser rate, than is shown for the population as a whole; while the
married persons have actually increased.

*The figures given relate to the County and County Borough.
EDUCATION.
From Table 12 it will be seen that of the total population in the County and
County Borough 19.8 per cent. of the males and 18.6 per cent. of the females are
attending educational establishments.
Of the children aged 6 to 13 inclusive, 3.5 per cent. of boys and 3.6 per cent.
of girls are not stated to be attending school.
·
·
At ages 15 to 19 inclusive, the persons still attending various kinds of educa-
tional establishments represent 13~5 per cent. of the rnales and 14,8 per cent. of
the females.


---

## Page 296

XX.
In the regional education area comprising the County Borough, 21.8 per cent.
of the males and 19.1 per cent. of the females are attending educational establish-
ments.
Of the children aged 6-13 inclusive, 1.8 per cent. of boys and 1.6 per cent.
of girls are not stated to be attending school. At ages 15-19 inclusive, the persons
still attending various kinds of educational establishments represent 18.7 per cent.
of the males and 14.1 per cent. of the females.
In endeavouring to draw conclusions from this table it will be necessary to
treat the Census :figures relating to persons attending technical schools with con-
siderable reserve, pending further investigation, since a n1arked difierence has been
found to exist between then1 and figures of pupils enrolled in technical schools
as published by the Ministry of Education. Further reference to this n1atter
will be made in the General Report on the Census relating to Northern Ireland
as a whole.
OCCUPATIONS .
. Proportion Occupied.-From Table XIII it will be seen that while for males
the percentage of the total population occupied has remained practically un-
changed since 1911, the proportion for females has slightly increased.
In 1926,
43.3 per cent. of the population of both sexes and all ages were occupied and thus
normally were wage earners. It should be noted that both employed and out-of-
work persons are included in the term "occupied." Of the males aged 12 years
and over, the proportion occupied has decreased slightly since 1911 : of the females,
the proportion has appreciably increased.

t The figures given relate to the County and County Borough as constituted at the date of each Census.
Numerically important Occupations.-When considering the grouping of
occupations into orders and sub-orders it should be remembered that this grouping
disregards the industries in which the persons may be engaged.
For exampl~,
the occupational order XXII (Transport Workers) includes porters, messengers,
carmen, etc., irrespective of industry, but excludes clerks, carpenters, navvies, etc.,
employed in the transport service. The latter occupations are included under
occupational orders XXVIII, XV, XVII, etc., respectively.
From Table XV it will be seen that about 46 per cent. of the occupied males
appear under the main group or order II, Agricultural Occupations. This order
contains mor~ than five times as many persons as the next in point of size-XXIII,
Commerce,· F1nance, and Insurance. The occupations of most numerical import-
ance among the males are : Farmers; Agricultural Labourers · and Farmers'
relatives assisting in the work of the farm.

Occupational Status and National Health Insurance.-A consideration of the
percentages in the following table, which has been compiled from Table 14, shows
that in the County and County Borough taken together between a third and a
fourth of the occupied males are either employers or workers on own account,
the proportion for occupied females being 1 in 7.
Of the employees, apprentices,
and out-of-work taken together, those who were returned as out of work at the
date of the Census represent 14 per cent. in the case of males and 7 per cent. in
the case of females.

It will be seen that in the County, Fishing and Agriculture directly provide
a livelihood for 55 per cent. of the occupied population; the Manufacturing
Industries (using that term in a wide sense) for 17 per cent. ; and Personal Service
(which includes service in hotels, restaurants, etc.) for 10 per cent.
The males
outnumber the females in all the groups except Professions, where the females are
slightly in excess, and Personal Service, where the females are more than four
times as numerous as the males.
In the County Borough, the Manufacturing Industries directly provide a
livelihood for 47 per cent. of the occupied population; Commerce and Finance
(vvhich includes the wholesale and retail distributive trades, banking, and insurance)
for 19 per cent. ; and Personal Service (which includes service in hotels, restaurants,
etc.) for 11 per cent. The main manufacturing industry is that of Clothing,
which providesfor 28 per cent. of the occupied population. The males outnumber
the females in all the groups except Manufacturing Industries and Professions,
where the females are in excess, and Personal Service, where the females are more
than twice as numerous as the males.
Table 16 shows the industrial distribution of the occupied population in greater
detail, separate figures being given for each industrial order and sub-order.

## Page 300

xxiv.
BIRTHPLACES.
Table 17 shows, by sex, the numbers of persons born in each of the counties
of Northern Ireland, other countries in the British Isles, overseas Dominions,
foreign countries, and at sea.
Of the total population .80.6 per ce1:1t: were born
in the County or County Borough, and 7.4 per cent. In the remaining parts of
Northern Ireland.
Only 0.6 per cent. were born outside the British Isles, and nearly
one third of these were born in the British Dominions and Colonies.
Persons born
in foreign countries numbered only 611, and 12 persons were born at sea.

(a) Including Birthplace not stated. In 1926 these numbered 20.
(b) Persons born in the Isle of Man and the Channel Islands have been included under this heading.
In 1926 they
numbered 5 persons born in the Isle of Man and 1 in the Channel Islands.
(c) The figures given relate to the County and County Borough as constituted at the date of each Census. A portion
of the County of Londonderry was transferred to the County of Antrim in 1915, consequent on the extension
of Portrush Urban District.
From Table XVIII it will be seen that the proportion of the population born
in Northern Ireland has gradually decreased since 1861, while the proportion born
in the Irish Free State has steadily increased and now forms almost 8 per cent. of
the whole.
The proportions for other parts of the British Isles and abroad have
also increased.
RELIGIOUS PROFESSIONS.
It will be seen from Table XIX that Roman Catholics and. Protestant
Episcopalians have increased in numbers, and now forii]. greater··-proportions of
the population than in 1911, while Presbyterians ancr Other and Not Stated
Denominations are proportionately less numerous. A slight decrease in numbers
is shown amongst Methodists, but their relative position remains unchanged.

portion of Londonderry was transferred to the County of Antrim in 1915, consequent on the extension of

t This :figure comprises 25,765 Church of Ireland, 1,899 Church of England, and 2 Episcopal Church of Scotland.
ORPHANHOOD.
Table 19 shows that, of the 44,905 children enumerated in the County and
County Borough at ages under 16, 5,258 have lost one or both parents.
This
figure excludes cases of orphaned children ·whose loss has been supplied by step-
parents or adoptive parents, and 117 cases in which no statement as to parents
was secured.

    Of the 5,258 orphans, 55 per cent. are fatherless and 36 per cent.
are motherless, the remaining 9 per cent. being orphaned by the death of both
parents. The high proportion of fatherless to motherless orphans is in part a
corollary of the high proportion of widows to widowers, though to some extent
it may be due to a possible tendency to regard the fathers of illegitimate children
as dead if their whereabouts are unknown.
INFIRMITIES.
The number of persons returned as both Deaf and Dutnb is 49, or 25 less than
the number returned in 1911. The Dumb Only numbered 8, which compares with
22 in 1911.
~

    The number returned as Blind has increased from 98 in 1911 to 187 in 1926.
The total number of persons who have been classified as Crippled is 441,
comprising 269 males and 172 females.
It has not been found possible to test
the reliability of this figure, and it should be accepted with due reserve, both on
account of the broad interpretation which necessarily had to be placed on the
term "crippled" for classification purposes, and because of a possible reluctance
on the part of some crippled persons to disclose their infirmity by stating it on
the Census schedule.
Of the 722 persons returned as mentally afflicted, 496 were enumerated in
Asylums and 41 in other institutions.
In 1911 the persons returned as Lunatic
or Idiot numbered 696, of whom 555 were enumerated in Asylums.
Particulars of the sex, age, and marital condition of persons suffering from
infirmities will be found in Table 20.

DEFI~TITIONS.-
1. Definitions of the terms" Area," "Popula.tion," "Houses," "Inhabited," "Private Families," "Rooms," and "Rateable
"Valuation" will be found on pages vi to viii,
2. The 19i1 figures throughout are for the County and its administrative divisions as constituted at the date of the Hl26 Census.
(See note on Administrative Divisions, page vi.)
3. Dots (.) indicate nil (0), and dashes (-) indicate that information cannot or is not intended to be given.
ARR.ANGEMENT.-1. Poor Law Unions are in alphabetical order, and, under each of these, Dispensary Districts n,re arranged in a, similar order.
2. District Electoral Divisions are arranged in alphabetical order under each Dispensary District.
3. The reference in the second column of the Table is to the serial number prefixed to t.he nam(l of en.cb Dist.riet Electoral
Division in the first column of Table 3.

DEFINITIONS.-
1. Definitions of the terms "Area," "Population," "Houses," "Inhabited," "Private Families," "Rooms," and "Ratea.ble
Valuation " will be found on pages vi to viii.
2. The 1911 figures throughout are for the County and its administmtive divisions as constituted at the date of the 1926 Census.
(See note on Administrative Divisions, page vi.)
3. Dots (.)indicate nil (0), and dashes(-) indicate that information cannot or is not intended to be given.
ARRANGEMENT.- 1. County Electoral Divisions are in alphabetical order, and, under each of these, District Electoral Divisions are arranged in
a similar order.
2. The reference in the second column of the Table is to the serial number prefixed to the name of each District Electoral
Division in the first column of Table 3.
ABBREVIATIONS.-C.E.D.=County Electoral Division; D.E.D.=District Electoral Division.

## Page 369

PREFACE.
This volu1ne has been prepared in accordance with the provisions of
Section 6 (1) of the Census Act (Northern Ireland), 1925.
The 1926 Census
statistics which it contains were compiled from the returns made as at midnight

    of the 18-19th April, 1926 : they supersede those in the Preliminary Report
published in August, 1926, and may be regarded as final.
The Census publications ~rill consist of:-
1.
SEVEN CouNTY VoLUMES, each similar in design and scope to the present
publication ..
2.
A GENERAL REPORT relating to Northern Ireland as a whole, covenng
in more detail the statistics shown in the County Volumes, and
containing in addition tables showing
(i.) the occupational distribution of persons engaged in each of
51 groups of industries ;
(ii.) the distribution of the foreign born population by nationality,
age, marital condition, and occupation ;
(iii.) the distribution of families of dependent children under 16
years of age, by age, sex, marital condition, and occupation of
parent;
(iv.) the occupational distribution of persons sufiering from infirmities.
3. A TOPOGRAPHICAL INDEX, dealing in separate alphabetical sections with
each type of area for which statistics are published in the County
series. The particulars given for each territorial unit include references
to the 6" Ordnance Survey maps and to Tables 3, 4, or 5 of the
relative County Volume.
The net prices for the above publications will be 5s. Od. for each County
Volume and lOs. Od. each for the General Report and the Topographical Index.
A complete set of these volumes may be obtained
directly
from the
Superintendent, H.M. Stationery Office, 15, Donegall Square \Vest, Belfast, at the
special inclusive price of £2 2s. Od. strictly net.
The Registrar-General is empowered by Section 6 (2) of the Census Act
(Northern Ireland), 1925, to prepare and supply from the Census returns, statistics
other than those published in the Census Reports at the request and cost of any
local authority or person. Any special demands for statistics more detailed than
those published (either as regards subject matter or area) can thus, as far as office
records permit, be satisfied. Requests for special compilation of Census statistics
should be addressed to the Registrar-General, who will be pleased to advise and
furnish an estimate of cost.
-
jULY, 1928.
GENERAL REGISTER OFFICE,
MuRRAY STREET,
BELFAST.
