## Page 372

vi.
PART I.
DEFINITIONS AND EXPLANATIONS OF
TERMS USED IN THIS VOLUME.
AREA.
The acreages given are as measured by the Ordnance Survey of 1899-1907.
They exclude
In Tables l, 3-5 (a) fore-shore and unnamed islands of less than one
rood in extent.
In Tables 3-5
(b) the larger rivers and lakes.
N .B.-While the larger rivers and lakes are not enclosed within townland
boundaries, they form part of adn1inistrative areas. The extent of these excluded
portions is shown for the county, county districts, and poor law unions in foot-
notes to the appropriate tables.
POPULATION.
The population comprises all persons, including Forces of the Crown, enumer-

    ated as at midnight of Sunday the 18th-19th April, 1926, together vvith those who,
not having been enumerated elsewhere, arrived in port or V\rere received into any
dwelling on the morning of the 19th ·April before the Census returns were collected.
The population assigned to each area is that actually present (as defined
above) within it, 'vhether as permanent residents or temporary visitors, persons
in port but on ship board being classified to the adjacent area.
In general these definitions apply to the population as enumerated at previous
censuses, and sho_wn in Table 2.
ADMINISTRATIVE
DIVISIONS.
The County is divided into ten county districts (four urban and six rural),
comprising 145 district electoral divisions, eleven of V\rhich are situate in urban
districts (see Table 3).
Of this latter group ten are coterminous with wards
bearing the same name.
Seven· poor law unions are contained within the County-six wholly and
one partly; the remaining portion of the divided union being situate in Fermanagh
County. The medical dispensary districts situate in the County number twenty-
eight (see Table 4).
Poor law unions and medical dispensary districts are,
respectively,
superintendent ·registrars' and registrars'
districts for the
registration of marriages, births, and deaths.
The County . is also. divided into twenty-seven ~ounty electoral divisions
(see Table· 5) ; . and into five regional education areas (see Table 12).
Apart from the reconstitution of county electoral divisions and district
electoral divisions in 1923, the changes which have taken place in the boundaries
of administrative divisions since 1911 are as follows:-.
(a) Trillick Rural District abolished and its area added to Omagh Rural
District (1st April, 1920).
·
·
(b) Strabane Poor Law Union reduced by the exclusion therefrom of the
County Donegal portion (1st April,.)92l).
(c) Wards in Dungannon Urban Districtreconstituted(llth November,1922).


---

## Page 373

vii.
PARLIAMENTARY
CONSTITUENCY.
The present parliamentary constituency vvas created by the Government
of Ireland Act, 1920, and comprises the administrative counties of Fermanagh
and Tyrone.
HOUSES.
1. The term '' House '' denotes
(a) each structure (except institutions) which stands alone or is separated
from another by a vertical party wall; and which is used or intended
partly or ·wholly for habitation ;
(b) each institution (viz., hospital, sanatorium, nursing home, infirmary,
workhouse, asylum, religious or charitable community,· residential
school or college, barracks or other premises under Naval, Military,
Air Force, or Police discipline, prison or other place of detention),
regardless of. the number of buildings within its precincts ;
(c) each vessel, caravan, tent, or other movable dwelling.
The 1911 figures for " Houses " do not include vessels, and each separate
building within the precincts of an institution was at that Census regarded as a
separate " House."
2. The terms" Boarding-House" and" Lodging-House" denote, in addition
to habitations so described, any private house containing three or n1ore boarders
or lodgers.
3. The term "Private House" denotes all habitations other than
(a)
dwellings within the precincts of an institution ;
(b)
hotels, boarding-houses, and other such communal dwellings ;
(c)
(d)
pren1ises in which three or more boarders, lodgers, or trade assistants
reside;
residences occupied by virtue of the tenant's employment, (except
rectories and manses, and dwellings belonging to but detached
from domestic establishments) ;
(e)
premises occupied by caretakers ;
(f)
ships, caravans, tents, and other movable dwellings ;
(g)
barns, stables, and out-houses occupied on Census night.
The term includes dwellings over shops except when these are occupied as
described at (c) and (d) above.
4.
The term "Inhabited" denotes actual residence of a person or persons,
on Census night.
" Uninhabited " houses, therefore, include those temporarily
vacant through the absence of the usual occupiers as well as those without t,enants.
Enumerators were instructed to omit from their enumeration empty houses in
such an advanced state of decay that there was little likelihood that they would
again be made fit for habitation. In this matter they were required to use their
own judgment.
ROOMS.
I. The rooms enumerated are the usual living rooms, including bedrooms
and kitchens, but excluding sculleries (i.e., rooms used only for washing, cleaning,
or cooking), pantries, cloakrooms, landings, closets or bathrooms, or any ware-
house, office or shop rooms.


---

## Page 374

viii.
2. For the purpose of classifying families by rooms occupied (Table 8),
shared-room occupations-i.e., those in which two or more families have
rooms in common,-have been rounded up to the next integral number of rooms.
Thus, three families occupying It, 2!, and 2-§- rooms in a 6-roomed house would
be shown as occupying 2, 3, and 3 rooms respectively. The extent of the rounding-
up process may be ascertained in a general way by reference to Table 7, in which
the actual number of rooms is given.
Generally, in cases of 1 room occupations,
all persons ·living therein, whether related or not, have been enumerated as one
Census family.
FAMILIES.
l. The term " Family " denotes
(a) The inhabitants of each institution (see l (b) of" Houses" definition),
hotel, boarding-house, lodging-house, or ship.
(b) Each person or group of persons occupying separately the whole, or
part of, any dwelling other than as described at (a).
In 1911 the inhabitants of premises described at (a) above were in certain
cases enumerated as two or more families.
.
2.
" Private Families " mean all families enumerated in " Private Houses"
(see 3 of " Houses " definition).
RATEABLE VALUATION.
The figures shown are those for service of the year ended 31st March, 1927,
and have been supplied by the Commissioner of Valuation.
Rateable heredita-
ments include lands, buildings, tolls, railways, tran1-roads, canals, fisheries,
turbaries, mines, quarries, waterworks, telegraphs, and telephones.
The figures
throughout exclude the valuation of water mains, telegraphs, telephones, fisheries,
and (in certain cases) waterworks, the amounts concerned being aggregated and
shown for the county, county districts, and poor law unions in foot-notes to the
appropriate tables.
EDUCATION.
The classification under educational establishments (which was assisted
by reference to lists supplied for Census purposes by the Ministry of Education)
is as follows :-
1. PuBLIC ELEMENTARY ScHooLs.
Day and evening pupils are grouped
together in this class, the question .. on the Census schedule not
requiring a separation as between day and evening in respect of
pupils attending public elementary schools.
2.
SECONDARY ScHooLs, including preparatory departments and preparatory
schools.
This heading relates to day pupils only, and the schools
are limited to those recognised by the Ministry of Education.
3.
TECHNICAL ScHOOLS.
The day pupils shown in 'this group comprise
those stated to be attending junior technical schools, day commercial
classes, and day classes for domestic economy and manual instruction
which were being held at certain centres in April, 1926. The evening
pupils comprise those stated to be attending technical schools or
classes in the evening.
4:.
OTHER EDUCATIONAL EsTABLISHMENTS (Day and Evening). These in-
clude secondary schools not recognised by the Ministry of Education,
universities,
training
colleges for teachers, agricultural schools,
commercial schools, home or private tuition, etc.


---

lX.
OCCUPATIONS.
The occupational classification is based on that used in the Reports on the
Census of England and Wales, 1921. *
It ~rill be seen that there are thirty-two orders.
Each of these covers the
sa111e occupational ground as the England and vVales orders.
Eleven of the
orders are div~ded __ into thirty-two sub-orders, each of which is comparable with
the corresponding I:!...ngland and Wales sub-order, vvith the following exceptions :-

Within the sub-orders are 341 occupational headings, evolved by processes
both of grouping and expansion from the England and \Vales headings, which
number 611.
INDUSTRIES.
The industrial classification is based on that used In the Reports on the
Census of England and Wales, 1921.*
It will be seen that there are twenty-two orders. Each of these cover the
same industrial ground as the England and Wales orders.
Nine of the orders
are divided into thirty-eight sub-orders, each of which is comparable ·with the
corresponding England and Wales subrorder, with the follo"ving exceptions:-
XVI.

Within the sub-orders are 226 industrial headings, evolved by processes both
of grouping and expansion from the England and Wales headings, which number
477.
The Industry table contained in this volume shows figures for orders and
sub-orders only.
BIRTHPLACES.
Persons stated to have been born in Ireland-the County name or "Irish
Free State" not being inserted-are assumed to have been born in the Irish Free
State. Where nationality is given but birthplace is not, it has been assumed that
the person was born in the country of which he or she is a national. All persons
of British nationality and unstated birthplace have been regarded as born in the
County of enumeration.
RELIGIOUS
PROFESSIONS.
The religious professions, other than Roman Catholic, Presbyterian, Church
of Ireland, and Methodist, have been classified under the headings set out at foot
of Table 18, separate mention being made of all professions containing 10 or more
adherents. The heading "Presbyterian" includes those who call themselves
by this name without qualification, together with those ~eturned as. meJ?bers of
the Established Church of Scotland.
Other Presbytenan denominations are
shown at the foot of the table.
Members of the Church of England and the
Episcopal Church of Scotland have not been included under Church of Ireland
except where stated. The latter group includes all persons returned as" Church"
or " Episcopalian."
* Full details of the English system of classification are published in the "Classification of Occupations,
Census, 1921,, and the "Classification of Industries, Census, 1921," to be purchased directly from H.M.S.O., or
through any Bookseller.


---

## Page 376

X.
ORPHANHOOD.
In compiling statistics under this head, step-parents, foster-parents, and
adoptive p~rents, so stated, have been treated in the same manner as natural
parents.
\Vhere the information as to one parent is stated as "not known,"
that parent has been assumed to be dead and the case dealt with accordingly.
\;vhere inforn1a tion as to neither parent is given, the case has been placed in the
"No Statement" group.
INFIRMITIES.
I. DEAF AND DuMB, DuMB, BLIND.
The figures given are in respect of
persons so returned, except ·where the answers indicate only partial infirmity, as
"nearly blind."
2.
CRIPPLED.
In addition to those persons specifically described as
"Crippled," the figures given include all cases in vvhich it is clearly indicated
that the person
(a) has suffered a total permanent loss of the use of arm. hand, leg, or
foot;
(b) is permanently crippled in such a degree as to prevent the free
pursuit of most ordinary occupations.
The following cases, treated as "Crippled," indicate the variety of the terms
used in answering the question on the Census schedule:-
Arm-deformed, disabled; hand-deformed; hip joint disease; knee-
decayed, tubercular arthritis in; leg-· disabled, diseased bone in,
infirm, short ; on crutch ; paralysis-infantile, nervous, of arms and
side, of legs and back, of spine; rheumatoid arthritis; spinal caries;
unable to walk.
3.
MENTALLY AFFLICTED.
All cases returned, whatever the degree of
affliction, are included in the tables.
The following terms were used in the
returns=-
Afflicted; feeble minded; imbecile; melancholia; mental debility; simple;
weak intellect.
4.
Persons suffering fro1n more than one infirmity have been classified to
that which appears most likely to constitute the greater, or greatest, disability,
infirmities being graded for this purpose from greatest to least, thus:-
Mentally afflicted; blind; crippled; deaf and dumb; ,dumb.
SPELLING OF PLACE
NAMES.
The spelling of place names follows that in the 1911 Census volumes,
except that corrections have been made to secure agreement with the spelling on the
Ordnance Survey maps where differences were revealed in the course of the work.
The spelling now may be regarded as generally following that adopted by the
Ordnance Survey Department.
·
CONVENTION
AS
TO
DOTS (.)
AND
DASHES (-}.
Throughout this volume dots (.) are used in tabular matter to indicate nil (0),
and dashes (-) are used to indicate that information cannot or is not intended
to be given.


---

## Page 377

xi.
CORRECTION
OF CENSUS
DATA.
Every effort has been made to eliminate, as far as practicable, errors and
inaccuracies from the Census data. The process of correction began with the
Enumerators, who received full instructions as to the action to be taken in cases
of error or on1ission ; it -vvas continued by a systen1 of check applied at each
stage of the office ·work; and \Vas completed by an intensive scrutiny and inter-
comparison of the tabulation sheets.
This scrutiny and con1parison revealed
a certain number of incongruities, '\;vhich, when material, ·were investigated and
corrected in the light of the facts disclosed.
Errors of a minor nature (i.e., such as
could not affect the statistical value of the Census Tables) ·were adjusted more or
less arbitrarily.
·


---

## Page 378

xii.
PART II.
STATISTICAL NOTES.
The definitions, etc. contained in Part I of this volume apply with equal
force to the contents of these notes and to the tables contained in Part III. Roman
numerals are used in this volume for the tables in Part II, and Arabic numerals
for the tables in Part III.
AREA.
The area of Tyrone County, exclusive of the larger rivers and lakes, is 1,218
square miles or 23.3 per cent. of the area of Northern Ireland.
POPULATION.
The population numbers 132,792 persons, the smallest recorded. The decrease
over the past 15 years amounts to 9,873 persons, or 6.9 per cent., a continuation
of the downward movement recorded at each Census enumeration from 1851
onwards. The average annual rate of decrease has, however, been lessening since

    1891, and that for the period 1911-1926 is the smallest since the beginning of the
downward trend.
(Figures for the earlier periods are shown in Table 2).

(a) Including the deaths of non-civilians of the County of Tyrone which occurred outside Northern Ireland.
It has not been found possible to estimate the number of these deaths at all closely, but from the War mortality
lists of soldiers from Ireland, which were compiled by the vVar Office from regimental records, it appears that
for the whole of Northern Ireland the non~civilian deaths which occurred outside the country numbered about
10,000 during the last intercensal period, exclusive of the deaths of regular soldiers stationed outside the country
at the date of the 1911 Census.
·
While comparable figures of excess of births over deaths are not available
for periods prior to 1871, it is evident that the actual net outw·ard movement during
the past 85 years has considerably exceeded the recorded decrease in population.
Complete information as to the size and direction of the emigration strearn is lack-
ing, but for the period 1871-1911 it can be stated that about four-fifths of the net
outward movement is accounted for by emigration to places abroad, and that
approximately 77 per cent. of the natives of County Tyrone living elsewhere in
Ireland were domiciled in the area which is now comprised by Northern Ireland.
From Table II it V\rill be seen that urban districts as a whole have increased
very slightly in population during the past 15 years, while rural districts have
in all cases decreased-the trend in each aggregate_ following that· exhibited in
the preceding :intercensal period.
The 1926 urban population constitutes 13.3

    per cent. of the whole, as against 12.2 per cent. in 1911, and 11.3 per cent. in 1901-
the percentages quoted relating to urban areas as constituted at the date of each
census. The greatest absolute decrease in population during the last intercensal
period occurred in Omagh Rural District, and the greatest relative decrease in
Castlederg Rural District.

(a) The :figures given relate to each county district as constituted at the date of the 1926 Census.
* Exclusive of 2 7,356 acres under the larger rivers and lakes.
The persons per acre rates vary in the urban districts between 16.8 in Dun-
gannon and 2.8 in Strabane. Such rates, however, should be used with reserve
in 1neasuring the degree of population density in urban areas, since the acreage of
these n1ay include widely differing proportions of waste land, land under pasture,
etc. In rural districts the rates vary from 0.1 to 0.2 persons per acre.
Tables 3, 4, and 5 provide more detailed information regarding the population
in the various sub-divisions of the County and incorporate figures of rateable
valuation.
The rateable valuation has increased from £466,040 to £468,593 over
the past :fifteen years, an increase of 0.5 per cent. as compared with a decrease of
6. 9 per cent. in population.

elector in respect of the occupation of land or premises (not being a dwelling-

with population will be dealt with more fully in the General Report.

While the total number of houses in the County decreased by 8.7 per cent.,
the number of inhabited houses decreased by 6.1 per cent. only-this being due to
a considerable reduction in the number of uninhabited houses, the 1926 figure for
which is the smallest recorded since 1871. The fact that the rate of decrease in
inhabited houses is less than that in population evidences a change in housing
conditions to which further reference is made in the comment on Table V below.
The number of houses under construction is the highest recorded since 1831.
Persons per Inhabited House and Persons per Fan;tily.
The average number
of persons per inhabited house, which showed a steady decrease from 1851 to 1911,
has again shown a decrease from 1911 to 1926. The average number of persons
per family has shown a corresponding decline.
In this connection it should
be noted that by " family " is meant Census family, which it will be seen from the
definition on page viii differs from the natural family of parents and children.
With regard to the habitation of houses by more than one fan1ily, there has
been little change during the past half c·entury, the number of families per 100
inhabited houses at each Census enumeration from 1871 to 1926 being as follows:

    103, 102, 101, 101, 100, 101.
Housing in County Districts.-Table V shows that there has been a decrease
in the total number of houses in all county districts but Dungannon Urban, where
a ~light increase is shown.
Comparison of this Table with Table II also shows
that since 1911 the proportion of inhabited houses to population has increased
in all county districts but Omagh Urban and Dungannon Rural, where the process
has been reversed, and Omagh Rural, where the p(j)sition is unchanged.

(a) The figures given relate to each county district as constituted at the date of the 1926 Census.
The extent of sub-letting in the county districts is best indicated by the factor
"number of private families per inhabited private house," which is readily obtain-
able from the figures of house accommodation contained in Table 7. It w-ill be
seen that the number of families per 100 private houses is 101 for the County, and
that there is little variation from this average. 99 per cent. of the families in the
County occupy a whole house each.
Of all the inhabited private houses in the County 99 per cent. are occupied
by single families and 51 per cent. contain 1 to 3 rooms.
Accommodation of Families and Persons.-The occupied room accommodation
in the County approximates to one room per person, the variation from this average
ranging between 1.13 in Cookstovvn Urban District and 0.81 in Castlederg Rural
District. A detailed analysis of the room accommodation, coupled with the size
of family, etc., appears in Table 8.
Over 25 per cent. of t}.e families in the County
are seen to occupy 3 rooms each, the family of 2 occurs most frequently, and the
most common combination consists of families of 2, each family occupying 2 rooms.

## Page 382

xvi.
Table VII, in conjunction ·with Table 8, will enable comparisons to be made
between the density o£ occupation in each county district. In these tables the
density of 2 persons and less per room has not been sub-divided, but this is not
intended to imply any judgment as to vvThat in fact constitutes adequate accom-
modation, since this matter obviously could not ce determined without a knowledge
of other factors, such as size of rooms, sanitary and \Vater provision, etc.
Sul:-
divisions other than those set out in summary form can readily le calculated from
the details given in the :first nine columns of Table 8.
In the County over 23 per cent. of the private family population occupy
premises at a density of more than 2 persons per room.
The average density of
occupation in urban districts as a whole 'is less than that in rural districts as a
whole, while the distribution of the population over the various densities differs
considerably for the respective aggregates, the proportion of the private family
population occupying premises at a density of more than 2 persons per room
being 13.9 per cent. in urban districts as against 24.5 per cent. in rural districts.
The greatest density of occupation is found in Castlederg R.ural District, where
34.3 per cent. of the private family population are housed at a density of more
than 2 persons per roon1.
INSTITUTIONS,
ETC.
The population in each o£ the types of inhabited buildings, etc., other than
private houses, is shown in Tables 9 and 10, together with the number of inmatf:ls
in respect of institutions. The institutions are not individually identified as in
1911, but the number of institutions in each category is given. The inmates of
the Poor Lavv

    Institutio~s number 377 as against 738 at the date of the 1911
Census. With respect to this comparison it should be noted that since 1911
Strabane Workhouse has been converted into a District Hospital, and the able·~
bodied paupers formerly accommodated therein transferred to Londonderry
Workhouse.
Persons on shipboard number 10 as against 4 in 1911. "Other
Premises" include police and military barracks, residential schools, premises
occupied by caretakers, etc.
SEXES,
AGES,
AND
MARITAL CONDITIONS.
Table 11 shows the distribution of the population of the County by sex, age.
and marital condition. The following tables summarise the position in 1926 and
the changes in the last intercensal period.

Excess of Males over Females.-Table 2 shows that the number of females

    per 1,000 males has fallen from 1,062 in 1821 to 978 in 1926, a preponderance of
males being first recorded in 1911. It will, however, be seen from Table 11 that

    the females still outnumber the males at ages 25-44 and 70 and over.
Distribution according to Age.-The decrease of 6.9 per cent. in the total

    population is the net result of decreases in age groups 0-44J 65-· 84, and 90 and
    over, and increases in age groups 45-64 and 85-89. Materials for an analysis
of the manner in which variations in the number of births and deaths from year to
year, the number of war deaths, and the movement of population have combined
to produce these changes in age distribution are not available: the effect has
been to reduce the average age of the population from approximately 32.4 years
in 1911 to 31.7 years at the present time.
Distribution by Marital Condition.-Table XII shows the percentage decrease
in the male and female population of each marital condition since 1911. It will be
noted that the single and widowed groups have decreased at a greater rate and the
married group at a lesser rate than is shown for the population as a whole (6.9
per cent.)

From Table 12 it will be seen that of the total population in the County 18~7
per cent. of the males and 19.1 per cent. of the females are attending educational
establishments.
Of the children aged 6 to 13 inclusive, 4.2 per cent. of boys and 4.3 per cent.
of girls are not stated to be attending school.
At ages 15 to 19 inclusive, the persons still attending various kinds of
educational establishments represent 11.1 per cent. of the males and 16.8 per
cent. of the females.


---

## Page 384

xviii.
There is little variation from the percentages given above, 1n each of the
:five regional education areas into which the County is divided.
In endeavouring to draw conclusions from this table it will be necessary to
treat the Census figures relating to persons attending technical schools with con-
siderable reserve, pending further investigation, since a marked difference has been
found to exist between them and figures of pupils enrolled in technical schools as
published by the Ministry of Education. Further reference to this matter will be
made in the General Report on the Census relating to Northern Ireland as a whole.
OCCUPATIONS.
Proportion occupied.-From Table XIII it will be seen that both for males
and females the percentage of the total population occupied is slightly lo·wer than

    in 1911. In 1926) 42.7 per cent. of the population of both sexes and all ages
were occupied, and thus normally were wage-earners. It should be noted that
both employed and out-of-work persons are included in the term " occupied."
Of the persons aged 12 years and over, the proportion occupied has slightly
decreased.

occupied population, etc., by age groups.
Numerically important Occupations.·-When considering the grouping of
occupations into orders and sub-orders it should be remembered that this grouping
disregards the industries in which the persons may be engaged.
For example,
the occupational order XXII (Transport Workers) includes porters, messengers,
carmen, etc., irrespective of industry, but excludes clerks, carpenters, navvies, etc.,
employed in the transport service.
The latter occupations are included under
occupational orders XXVIII, XV, XVII, etc., respectively.
From Table XV it will be seen that almost 67 per cent. of the occupied males
appear under the main group or order II, Agricultural Occupations.
The occu-
pations of most numerical importance among the males are: Farmers; Farmers'
Relatives assisting in the work of the farm ; and Agricultural Labourers.
·
Of the occupied females nearly 74 per cent. are included under the orders
XXVII, Personal Service ; II, Agricultural Occupations ; and XII, Textile
Workers ; the most important occupations numerically being Domestic Servants ;
Farmers; and Farmers' Relatives assisting in the work of the farm.
Table 13 shows by sex the number of persons included under each heading of
the full occupational classi:fica tion.

## Page 386

XX.
Of all the occupied population 25.6 per cent. of the males and 41.4 per cent.
of the females are returned as being entitled to benefits under the 1\f ational Health
Insurance Acts. For employers and workers on own account of all ages, taken
together, the percentages are 0.9 per cent. of the males and 0. 7 per cent. of the
females, and for employees, apprentices, and out of work, taken together, they
are 41.6 per cent. of the males and 53.2 per cent. of the females.
Of the unoccupied, including retired, 2.2 per cent. of the males and 0.8 per
cent. of the females are returned as entitled to benefits.

Fishing and Agriculture directly provide a livelihood for 57 per cent. of the
occupied population ; and employ betv1een three and four times as many persons
as are engaged in the Manufacturing Industries, which come next in numerical
importance.
The males outnumber the females in all the groups, except the Professions,
where the females are slightly in excess, and Personal Service, ·where the females
are nearly four times as numerous as the males.
Table 16 shows the industrial distribution of the occupied population in greater
detail, figur~s being giveJ?. for each industrial order a~d sub-order.
BIRTHPLACES.
Table 17 shows, by sex, the numbers of .persons born in each of the counties
of ·Northern Ireland, other countries in the British Isles, overseas Dominions,
foreign countries, and at sea.
Of the total population of County Tyrone, 85 per
cent. were boni in the County, and 7 per cent. in the re:rnaining parts of Northern
Ireland. Only 0.53 per cent. were born outside the British Isles, and· nearly one
third of these were born in the British Dominions and Colonies.
Persons born in
foreign countries numbered 496, and 6 persons ~ere borl1 at sea.

From Table XVIII it will be seen that the proportion of the population born
in Northern Ireland, which novv stands at 92.3 per cent., has decreased slightly
from census to census since 1861, while the proportions for other parts of the
British Isles and abroad each show a gradual increase.
RELIGIOUS
PROFESSIONS.
It will be seen from Table XIX that, while Roman. Catholics and Other and
not stated Denominations each form a greater proportion of the population than
in 1911, it is only among the last named that an increase in numbers has been
recorded. The greatest absolute decrease occurred amongst Roman Catholics,
and the greatest relative decrease amongst Protestant Episcopalians.

---

## Page 388

xxii.
ORPHANHOOD.
Table 19 shows that of the 41,215 children enumerated in the County at
ages under 16, 4,778 have lost one or both parents. This figure excludes cases of
orphaned children whose loss has been supplied by step-parents or adoptive parents,
and 149 cases in which no statement as to parents was secured.
Of the 4,778
orphans, 53 per cent. are fatherless and 40 per cent. are motherless, the remaining
7 per cent. being orphaned by the death of both parents. The high proportion
of fatherless to motherless orphans is in part a corollary of the high proportion of
wido·ws to widowers, though to some extent it may be due to a possible tendency
to regard the fathers of illegitimate children as dead if their whereabouts are
unknown.
INFIRMITIES.
The number of persons returned as both Deaf and Dumb is 46, or 25less than
the number returned in 1911. The Dumb Only numbered 19, which compares
with 27 in 1911.

    The number returned as Blind has increased from 117 in 1911 to 236 in 1926.
The total number of persons who have been classified as Crippled is 553,
comprising 329 males and 224 females.
It has not been found possible to test the
reliability of this figure, and it should be accepted with due reserve, both on
account of the broad interpretation which necessarily had to be placed on the
term "crippled" for classification purposes, and because of a possible reluctance
on the part of some crippled persons to disclose their infirmity by stating it on
the census schedule.
Of the 959 persons returned as mentally afflicted 770 were enumerated in
the District Lunatic Asylum and 20 in other institutions. In 1911, the persons
returned as Lunatic or Idiot numbered 1,013, of whom 859 were enumerated in
the Asylum.
Particulars of the sex, age, and marital condition of persons suffering from
infirmities will be found in Table 20.

DEFINITIONS.-
1. Definitions o:f the terms "Area/' ":Population," "Houses," "Inhabited," ":Private Families," "Rooms," and "Rateable
Valuation " will be found on pages vi to viii.

    2. The 1911 figures throughout are for the County and its administrative divisions as con13tituted at the date of the 1926 Census.
(See note on .Administrative Divisions, page vi).
3. The Towns shown in this table in italics have no defined boundaries, but comprise groups of 50 houses and upwards, as
determined by the enumerator.
4. Dots (.) indicate nil (0), and dashes (-) indicate that information cannot or is not intended to be given .
.ARRANGEMENT.-!. Urban Districts are in alphabetical order followed by Rural Districts in a similar order.
2. District Electoral Divisions are arranged in alphabetical order under each Urban and Rural District.
3. Within each District Electoral Division, Townlands are shown in alphabetical order under the Dispensary District in which
they are situated. For entire Dispensary Districts see Table 4.
" 4. Where references to other D.E.D.'s are given in footnotes the D.E.D. reference numbers are added in brackets .
.ABBREVIATIONS.- D.=Dispensary District; D.E.D.=District Electoral Division.

DEFINITIONS. -1. DefinitionB of the terms ''Area," "Population," "Houses," "·Inhabited," "Private Families," "Rooms," and "Rateable
Valuation,. will be found on pages vi to viii.
2. The 1911 figures throughout are for the County and its administrative divisions as constituted at the elate of the 1026 Oonsus.
(See note on Administrative Divisions, page vi).$
3. Dots (.)indicate nil (0), and dashes(-) indicate that information cannot or is not intended to be given.
ARRANGEMENT.-I. Poor Law U:nions are in alphab~tical order, and, under each of these, Dispensary Districts are arranged in a simihir order.
.
2. District Electoral Divisions are arranged in alphabetical order under each Dispensary District.

-1. Definitions of the terms" Area," "Population," "Houses," "Inhabited," "Private Families," "Rooms,'! and '*Rateable
Valuation " will be found on pages vi to viii.
2. The I911 :figures throughout axe for the County and its administrative divisions as constituted at the date of the I926 Census.
(See note on Administrative Divisions, page vi).
3. Dots(.) indicate njl (0), and dashes(-) indicate that information cannot or is not intended to be given.
ARRANGEMENT.- I. County Electoral Divisions are in alphabetical order, and, under each of these, District Electoral Divisions are arranged in
a similar order.
2. The reference in the second colull!n of the Table is to the serial number prefixed to the name of each District Electoral
Division in the first column of Table 3.
AB:BREVIATIONS.-C.E.D.=County Electoral Division; D.E.D.=District Electoral Division.
